title: Scaling Social Protection and Employment Support to Unlock Jobs for Youth and People in Poverty
body:
Summary
The Challenge
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
Country Examples
Lessons Learned
Results Narrative
Protection for the Poorest
Scaling Social Protection and Employment Support to Unlock Jobs for Youth and People in Poverty
Credit: ©Rama George-Alleyne
Summary
This Results Narrative highlights how the World Bank supports job creation for the poorest by scaling up proven solutions that address capacity constraints, evidence gaps, fragmentation, and limited private sector engagement. Through programs in Bangladesh, Benin, Kenya, Zambia, and elsewhere, it strengthens delivery systems, embeds evidence into operations, and fosters public-private collaboration. These efforts have increased incomes, reduced poverty, and improved employment outcomes—especially for women and youth—reinforcing the SP500 goal to reach 500 million people including 250 million women by 2030.
The Challenge
Jobs offer a pathway out of poverty, empower women, give hope to younger generations, and underpin economic growth. Yet for many poor and vulnerable people, especially women and youth, access to formal employment remains out of reach. Private sector growth and public sector reforms are critical to creating more jobs over the long-term, but many people cannot afford to wait.
Countries are increasingly investing in two complementary tools that deliver jobs now: economic inclusion programs and youth employment interventions. These programs support microentrepreneurs who lack access to traditional markets and capital and create pathways for workers facing barriers to wage jobs.
Economic inclusion programs combine income support with business capital, entrepreneurship and skills training, financial literacy, coaching, and market linkages to help poor and vulnerable people start microenterprises and engage in self-employment. These programs boost incomes, build assets and savings, and improve household well-being, especially for women.
Youth employment interventions, such as work-based learning initiatives or apprenticeships, equip young people with practical skills, on-the-job experience, and employer connections.
These approaches are central to the World Bank’s commitment to reach 500 million more people with social protection and employment support by 2030 (SP500), helping the poorest build resilience and self-reliance.
Economic inclusion programs and youth employment interventions deliver a high return on investment by increasing earnings and opportunities today and sustaining gains over time. In the Sahel, returns reached 163-248 percent; in India, long-term studies show every $1 invested in the Graduation approach yielded $3.8 in benefits by year 10. While youth employment programs vary widely, evidence suggests relatively high impacts in developing country contexts—especially with strong targeting, adaptation to local conditions, and robust delivery mechanisms. Yet scaling remains difficult due to persistent barriers: capacity constraints, evidence gaps, fragmentation, and limited private sector engagement.
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
To address persistent constraints, the World Bank is strengthening delivery systems, embedding evidence into operations, and fostering public-private collaboration. Insights from diverse experiences shape its approach to expanding jobs for poor workers who cannot wait. Knowledge spillovers from economic inclusion programs in other regions have informed the design and scaling of interventions, allowing adaptation of successful approaches to local contexts.
Strengthening institutional capacity to deliver at scale: Many governments need technical assistance and operational support to design, implement, and sustain complex economic inclusion and youth employment programs. Investments in staff training, delivery systems, and policy dialogue—through platforms such as Partnership for Economic Inclusion (PEI) and Solutions for Youth Employment (S4YE)—build government ownership and program sustainability.
PEI has become a global center of excellence for technical know-how, knowledge creation and dissemination, and good practices that support the design and scale-up of economic inclusion programs. Leveraging the World Bank’s convening role, S4YE engages more than 120 non-governmental and civil society organizations and private sector partners to promote youth labor market outcomes through shared resources and best practices.
Generating evidence to accelerate learning: Embedding operational research into delivery strengthens design and improves value for money. The PEI’s Impact Collaborative and the Gender Innovation Lab integrate impact evaluations and real-time learning into operations, building an evidence base to guide program design and inform future investments.
Fostering public-private collaboration: The World Bank is incorporating public-private partnership models and innovative financing tools to improve relevance and outcomes. In Kenya, the National Youth Opportunities Towards Advancement (NYOTA) program uses performance-based contracts that reward private training providers for  job placements, retention, and adherence to quality standards—aligning incentives with employment goals.
Knowledge sharing to strengthen outcomes: As a convener, the World Bank promotes dissemination of good practices and peer-to-peer learning through platforms such as the Jobs for Youth in Africa Community of Practice, South-South Learning Forum (SSLF), and the Jobs for the Poor Impact Program—supporting more effective design and delivery at scale.
Dynamic Knowledge Sharing to Promote Peer Learning
Building on the World Bank’s experience with large-scale peer learning, the Jobs for the Poor Impact Program launched in April 2025 to help 11 countries scale economic inclusion programs through structured action-oriented learning. It draws on lessons from a recent SSLF, which brought together more than 270 government leaders and practitioners from nearly 60 countries to exchange practical experiences in designing and implementing economic inclusion programs. By linking global learning platforms with targeted country support, these initiatives strengthen systems, align stakeholders, and accelerate the adoption of evidence-based approaches to increase income and employment for the poorest.
In parallel, the Jobs for Youth in Africa Community of Practice was launched with the theme “Jobs for the Poor.” Led by governments, policy makers, and practitioners across Sub-Saharan Africa, it promotes youth employment by sharing experiences, fostering dialogue, and encouraging peer-to-peer learning and joint action, with technical support from the World Bank. Together, these platforms help countries move from shared knowledge to results while reinforcing the SP500 goal.
Country Examples
Zambia
Economic Inclusion for Women in Rural Areas
Credit: ©Sam DCruz
Rural women in Zambia face systemic barriers to meaningful activity, including limited access to training, markets, and capital—leaving many dependent on subsistence livelihoods and vulnerable to shocks.
To address these challenges, the Government of the Republic of Zambia, with World Bank support, launched the Supporting Women’s Livelihoods (SWL) program under the Girls’ Education and Women’s Empowerment and Livelihoods Project. Nearly 145,000 women have received a package of support that includes life and business skills training; six months of group-based mentorship; and a one-time productivity grant for income-generating activities. Participants also join savings groups to build financial resilience and access small loans.
The program combines diagnostics—identifying barriers such as weak linkages to structured value chains—with capacity building to strengthen implementation and market access.
A randomized impact evaluation conducted in partnership with the Africa Gender Innovation Lab across 298 communities in 10 districts tracked outcomes of the program over four years. The SWL intervention reduced extreme poverty by 30 percent, increased household incomes by 62 percent, improved business profits by 80 percent, and raised household savings by 234 percent, on average. Also, household consumption rose by 38 percent. Impacts were strongest among women receiving the full package of training, mentorship, grants, and savings facilitation. The program demonstrated a high rate of return, with benefit-cost ratios estimated between 133 and 1001 percent for the full package. Based on these results, Zambia is scaling under the intervention’s second phase, increasing the total coverage to 200,000 women.
Benin
A Gender-Responsive Economic Inclusion Approach
Credit: ©Dave Primov
Source: SCOM ProDIJ/AnpE July 2025
"I am enjoying a new professional experience that I had never dreamed of."
Koumo Oumarou, Textile operator at GTC/Glo-Djigbé Industrial Zone
Young women in Benin face barriers in skills development and employment, including wage disparities, limited access to financial services, risks of gender-based violence, and social norms that restrict entry into the labor market.
With World Bank support, the Government of Benin launched the Azôli program (“pathway to employment” in Fon) under the Youth Inclusion Project in 2021. Since its national rollout, Azôli has reached over 46,000 youth, of which 45 percent are women. The program introduced gender-responsive interventions to address barriers preventing women from entering wage employment, particularly in male-dominated sectors. Capacity building for Benin’s National Employment Agency and municipal staff included training on labor market dynamics, employment policies, jobseeker counseling, employer relations, gender-based violence, and gender equity. Staff delivering the program became more sensitive to women’s needs, improving service responsiveness. The program also offered community-based childcare for parents with small children, most of whom are women.
These adaptations substantially increased women’s interest and participation—from 20 percent to 45 percent—particularly in northern regions where restrictive gender norms are more prevalent. By combining innovation and capacity building, Azôli is laying a foundation for long-term empowerment and resilience for women and youth. Without World Bank support, young women in Benin might have continued to face significant barriers to employment, affecting their economic independence and social empowerment.
Bangladesh
Advancing Economic Inclusion for Youth
Credit: ©Jahangir Alam Onuchcha
In 2023, nearly 40 percent of Bangladesh’s youth (ages 15-24) were not in education, employment, or training (NEET) (Bangladesh Bureau of Statistics 2023), with women more likely to be NEET and facing greater barriers to labor force participation. Many young workers are in the informal economy with poor working conditions, low wages, and limited advancement opportunities. These dynamics constrain poverty reduction.
In 2021, with World Bank support, the Government of Bangladesh launched a project to deliver economic inclusion support to more than 175,000 low-income urban and peri-urban youth affected by the COVID-19 pandemic. PEI facilitated knowledge exchange on linking services and demonstrating the fiscal and delivery benefits of multifaceted programs, which helped build understanding and political support for economic inclusion. Evidence-based policy notes informed dialogue on “graduation” from safety net programs.
Many young adults in Bangladesh face significant hurdles in accessing quality employment, which the IDA-financed RAISE program helps to remove. [Source: Nushin Subhan/World Bank]
To date, more than 32,000 youth have enrolled across 26 trades via an apprenticeship track. Combining on-the-job learning with life skills training, the program reports that 90 percent of graduates secured wage or self-employment in sectors such as ready-made garments, beauty services, welding, and electrical installation and repair. Over 78,000 youth have strengthened microenterprises through technical, business management, and life skills training; coaching and mentoring; and access to finance. In addition, 50,000 COVID-affected microentrepreneurs received recovery loans and risk management and business continuity training. Women’s participation has surpassed targets, with women accounting for 70 percent of participants. While results are promising, these gains should be interpreted within the broader context of Bangladesh’s labor market, where youth unemployment and informality remain persistent challenges. Building on its success, the Government of Bangladesh plans to expand the program across the country, aiming to reach an additional 200,000 youth and microentrepreneurs and 250,000 returning migrants.
Lessons from this initiative inform program design in other countries, especially gender-responsive design addressing specific barriers facing women.
Kenya
Youth Employment in Kenya: A Growing Workforce
Facing Limited Opportunities
Credit: ©Georgina Goodwin
Kenya’s rapidly growing youth population presents an opportunity for economic development, but job creation has not kept pace. Many young people lack market-relevant technical and soft skills, access to finance and networks, and opportunities for quality jobs—leading to unemployment, underemployment, or vulnerable work.
Launched in 2016, a World Bank-supported intervention provided a package of support to increase employment and earnings for Kenyan youth aged 18 to 29. It addressed skills mismatches through training and work experience in the private sector and offered start-up grants, business development services, and mentoring to self-employed youth and entrepreneurs.  A business plan competition targeted 750 high-growth young entrepreneurs with larger grants and mentoring support to start or expand ventures and create jobs.
The comprehensive package was successful in improving Kenyans’ employability, employment, and earnings. Nearly 150,000 youth benefited directly from either the employability or entrepreneurship track, which achieved an average retention rate of 85 percent. For youth in the employability track, earnings increased by 40.3 percent after training and internship placement.
Among those receiving grants or Business Development Support, nearly 90 percent were employed or self-employed six months later, and earnings were 50 percent higher compared to pre-participation levels. Impact evaluations confirmed strong outcomes of the entrepreneurship component: beneficiaries were over 27 percent more likely to start a business, with higher business survival (85 percent) compared to non-beneficiaries (74 percent). They also reported significantly higher sales (91 percent) and profits (90 percent).
Finally, the project’s entrepreneurship component also contributed to indirect job creation—2,700 jobs were created through the 750 business plan winners plus an estimated 30,000 additional jobs through beneficiaries of small grants. This underscores the value of comprehensive, demand-driven approaches to youth employment and highlights a successful model of leveraging public-private partnerships to help lift young people out of extreme poverty.
These outcomes reflect real progress among program participants, though youth unemployment and underemployment continue to pose systemic challenges across Kenya’s labor market.
The new National Youth Opportunities Towards Advancement project will scale up the intervention’s entrepreneurship and employability tracks, with a target of reaching 800,000 youth across the country. It also introduces innovations such as savings schemes for young workers in the informal sector, a maternity insurance scheme for young mothers, and support for refugees and host communities in selected counties.
Lessons Learned
Achieving the World Bank’s SP500 target—reaching 500 million more people with social protection and employment support by 2030—demands bold, inclusive action to expand access to quality jobs and income-generating opportunities for the poorest, especially women and youth. Economic inclusion and youth employment programs are resulting in real impact: boosting incomes, helping people transition to dignified employment, and enabling them to move from crisis to resilience. However, challenges remain in replicating results at scale, particularly in countries with limited delivery systems or persistent labor market barriers.
Across diverse country experiences, several key lessons are shaping the World Bank’s approach to advancing economic inclusion for women and youth employment:
Government ownership and delivery systems are foundational to sustain impact. In Zambia, investments in diagnostics, staff training, and support for community savings groups enabled the SWL program to empower nearly 145,000 women and reduce extreme poverty by 30 percent, laying the groundwork for a national rollout to 200,000. In Benin, targeted capacity building for government agencies led to more responsive services and increased women’s participation in the Azôli program.
Embedding evidence into operations strengthens program design and drives scale. In Zambia, a three-year impact evaluation of the SWL program revealed substantial gains in income, savings, and business profits, helping demonstrate the value for money and guiding scale-up. In Bangladesh, implementation insights supported by PEI helped integrate economic inclusion approaches into national programs, resulting in high employment outcomes and informing the design of two government pilot initiatives.
Peer learning fosters practical adaptation and momentum for reform. The SSLF and the newly launched Jobs for the Poor Impact Program help countries tailor global lessons to local contexts, reinforcing government-led reforms. The Jobs for Youth in Africa Community of Practice has brought together African governments to tackle youth unemployment through collaborative learning and joint action.
Public-private partnerships improve program relevance and sustainability. In Kenya, the program linked young people to internships, grants, and business development support—resulting in a 50 percent increase in earnings and 90 percent employment retention. Its successor, NYOTA, scales this model through performance-based contracts with private training providers and includes innovations like savings mechanisms and maternity insurance to reach more marginalized youth, including those in refugee-hosting communities.
Reaching scale requires deeper investments, stronger partnerships, and sustained political will. By delivering jobs now, we can unlock opportunity, restore dignity, and ensure lasting prosperity for those most in need.

title: Tackling Learning Poverty with Effective Teaching and Focused Student Support
body:
Summary
The Challenge
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
Country Examples
Lessons Learned
Results Narrative
No Learning Poverty
Tackling Learning Poverty with Effective Teaching and Focused Student Support
Credit: ©David Perez Vazquez
Summary
The World Bank Group (WBG) is addressing persistent constraints to foundational learning—such as outdated curricula, inadequate teacher preparation, and weak institutional capacity—by supporting systemic reforms that improve teaching quality and student support. Examples from Côte d’Ivoire, Morocco, and Viet Nam illustrate how investments in structured pedagogy, teacher pipelines, and professional development are helping close learning gaps. Lessons learned emphasize the need for greater focus on initial teacher education and sustained support across the teacher career lifecycle for impact.
The Challenge
Effective teaching and focused student support in emerging markets are key to creating strong foundational skills and competencies required for the job market. The WBG works directly with governments and private organizations—through technical support, policy advice, advocacy, investments, and partnerships—to provide everyone with the opportunity to receive the education they need to succeed in today’s world.
Learning outcomes are alarmingly low in many countries across the world. An estimated 70 percent of children in low- and middle-income countries suffer from learning poverty—the inability of children to read and understand a short age-appropriate text by the end of primary school; even among adults, functional illiteracy remains a significant concern. Efforts to develop foundational skills and reduce learning poverty are hindered by several factors, including gaps in the essential components of effective education, such as curricula, teaching materials, and teacher preparation and professional development.
Teachers are often unequipped with the core knowledge and skills for teaching in real classroom settings, which are often overcrowded and under-resourced. Teaching is also often undervalued due to low pay and difficult working conditions, resulting in recruitment and retention difficulties. Chronic underinvestment in initial teacher education weakens student learning outcomes and can lead to graduates who are not job ready. Moreover, continuing professional development programs and career frameworks often fail to effectively improve teachers' skills, motivation, or ability to meet students' needs.
Online initial teacher education programs pose yet another challenge. While the internet opens doors to education and training for rural residents and working adults, the quality of online certifications and degrees is often inconsistent. Further, such programs do not always provide teachers with practical skills to be effective in the classroom, usually honed through field placement experiences. Investments in quality assurance can help enhance the effectiveness of initial teacher education programs.
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
The WBG aims to help countries improve foundational skills and competencies by addressing gaps in the essential components of effective education, including strengthening curricula, teaching and learning materials, initial teacher education, and continuous professional development offered by both public and private providers.
The WBG uses evidence-based strategies, including recommendations from the Global Education Evidence Advisory Panel (GEEAP), to co-design projects with national governments to tackle learning poverty. The GEEAP 2023 report highlights two cost-effective approaches that improve learning—effective teaching through structured pedagogy and focused student support through targeted teaching. The first approach involves a strategic and coherent package of investments that align student learning materials, teacher guides, initial teacher education, and continuing professional development for effective teaching based on the science of how children learn. The second approach ensures lessons meet students where they are in their development of knowledge, skills, and competencies, addressing diverse classroom and catch-up needs.
The WBG also works to enhance the quality of initial teacher education and continuing professional development programs so that teachers—from their first day—have the core knowledge and skills for teaching, develop a strong professional identity, and stay up to date with curriculum changes and new technologies. Key principles and policy actions to help countries build effective and sustainable initial teacher education systems include attracting and selecting strong teacher candidates, providing impactful program content and field experience, aligning institutions for responsive, efficient, and quality-driven initial teacher education, and quality assurance processes. In addition, the WBG through IFC works closely with and invests in private universities that offer initial teacher education programs for future teachers across emerging markets.
As a complementary approach, IFC also supports education technology companies that develop learning management systems, customizable content, assessment frameworks, and other tools to efficiently teach and learn. These systems offer engaging content, well-designed exercises, and assessments to enhance teaching and learning experience. Teachmint, a global ed-tech company and IFC client, operates learning management systems for schools, universities, and coaching centers, reaching 20 million users in 50 countries. To reach more students, IFC has developed an upstream-enabled advisory program called Digital for Tertiary Education Program, already implemented with 24 universities, to support the digital transformation of higher education institutions and enhance access to learning for students, faculty, and administrative staff.
From Learning Poverty to Job Creation
Tackling learning poverty through effective teaching and focused student support is not only a moral and social imperative but also a powerful engine for direct and indirect job creation. Education and skills are critical for more and better jobs: individuals with higher levels of education consistently experience better employment prospects, higher earnings, and greater resilience in the face of economic shocks. The WBG shows that access to quality education equips learners with foundational skills essential for work and life, laying the groundwork for a more productive workforce and sustained economic growth. Conversely, ineffective early learning is unfolding into a job crisis, as millions of young people leave school without the basic literacy and numeracy skills needed to participate in the labor market or adapt to new technologies. This skills gap not only limits opportunities but also constrains employers’ ability to find qualified workers, stifling innovation and economic dynamism.
By investing in effective teaching through structured pedagogy, focused student support through targeted teaching, and technological innovation, education systems can ensure that all children acquire foundational skills, reducing learning poverty and unlocking pathways to employment. These interventions have direct effects—such as creating jobs for teachers, school leaders, support staff—and indirect effects, such as building a skilled workforce that attracts investment, fosters entrepreneurship, and drives job creation across sectors. As the World Bank’s recent evaluations emphasize, addressing the learning crisis is essential for breaking the cycle of poverty and exclusion. It also enables countries to harness the full potential of their human capital for inclusive growth and shared prosperity.
Country Examples
Morocco
Education Support Program
Credit: ©World Bank
The expansion in access to basic education (including preschool) over the last several decades in Morocco has been commendable. Yet nearly 60 percent of children at late primary age (2023) are still not proficient in reading (although an improvement from 2016 when it was 66 percent). Effective initial teacher education and professional development are key policy reforms helping to improve Morocco’s education system.
The World Bank has helped Morocco strengthen and support a pipeline of teachers for improved student learning with new hiring and incentive practices, teacher selection, initial teacher education, and induction approaches. A Bank-supported program introduced new teaching methods such as targeted teaching and explicit instruction. Initial teacher education has been restructured at scale into a more selective three-year undergraduate qualification followed by a competitive two-year induction program for new teachers. Additionally, a new professional development model for in-service teachers includes more systematic and individualized coaching, mentoring, virtual platforms, and on-site sessions, offering greater opportunities for professional growth.
The program also enhances the teacher’s role in the classroom through the Pioneer School approach. An objective of the Pioneer Schools is to improve students’ foundational skills across grades one to six in core subjects in cost-effective ways. Participating schools receive comprehensive support, including digital tools, lesson plans, focused student support, targeted teaching, continuous assessment, and increased budgets. Using simple formative assessments and engaging and interactive curricula, teachers collect systematic data for every student. After six weeks of targeted teaching, an assessment revealed catch-up of at least one to two years of schooling. The approach also implements structured, simplified teaching aids across several curriculum subjects, demonstrating an important shift from curriculum coverage toward a mastery approach, and providing teachers with tools for instruction, formative assessment, and enriched curricula.
A 2023–2024 impact study shows significant improvements, with students in Pioneer Schools outperforming 82 percent of their peers in comparable schools and dropout rates falling to less than 1 percent. This academic year, the program has reached 2,626 public primary schools totaling 1.3 million pupils (30 percent of all primary school students), 45,000 teachers, and 562 inspectors, with scale-up plans underway.
By bridging the gap between education policies and classroom practices, World Bank support is advancing reforms to improve education quality in a scalable and sustainable manner and provide foundational skills crucial for thriving in school, the labor market, and throughout productive lives.
Côte d’Ivoire
Strengthening the Basic Education System
Credit: ©Olivier Girard
Weak institutional capacity, compounded by uneven teacher distribution, gender imbalances, and insufficient infrastructure, has long plagued Côte d’Ivoire’s education system, holding back adequate building of foundational skills essential for later success in the job market.
A recent World Bank intervention focuses on two core strategies: promoting effective teaching through a structured pedagogy package and providing focused student support. The first approach integrates curriculum reform, teacher guides, student textbooks, and continuing professional development into a cohesive package. This equips teachers with sequenced lesson plans and continuous support, enabling them to adopt effective, student-centered methods. The second approach ensures lessons are tailored to students’ learning levels, with remedial classes and catch-up programs offered where necessary to help bridge students’ learning gaps.
The intervention also strengthens teachers’ capacity by supporting the implementation of a national teacher professional development strategy and incentivizing regionalized recruitment reforms to address disparities in teacher allocation. Broader systemic improvements include enhancing the education management information system and introducing performance-based contracts for regional and provincial education directorates. These efforts aim to improve equity, reduce teacher absenteeism, and address barriers to geographic mobility, particularly for women teachers.
By focusing on core literacy and numeracy, activities are laying the foundation for inclusive growth in Côte d’Ivoire. From June 2022 to December 2024, the net access rate for grade 1 increased from 75 percent to 82 percent, while the percentage of grade 3 students meeting the minimum reading proficiency threshold increased from 19 to 26 percent. Early results are encouraging and continued investment in teacher training and curriculum reform are needed to sustain gains.
In addition, the MIGA-housed WBG Guarantee platform helped Côte d'Ivoire implement a first-of-its-kind debt-for-development swap—an innovative financing solution that improves debt terms while funding critical development needs. IBRD provided a €240 million Policy-Based Guarantee to support a €400 million commercial loan with favorable terms. By securing better terms, Cote d’Ivoire will generate about €60 million in budget resources over five years, some of which will support the Ministry of Education’s goal to catalyze the construction of over 37 new lower secondary schools and benefit 30,000 students across the country.
Viet Nam
Enhancing Teacher Education
Credit: ©World Bank
The World Bank’s technical assistance and convening power played a pivotal role in shaping Viet Nam’s approach to teachers’ continuous professional development. Beyond financing, the World Bank’s support was instrumental in mobilizing international and national expertise, fostering institutional learning, and building sustainable systems for school-based continuous professional development across the country. Through technical assistance, the World Bank helped the Ministry of Education and Training and eight Lead Teacher Training Universities (LTTUs) to develop and operationalize new policies, management tools, and quality assurance mechanisms. Notably, the World Bank convened experts from several countries with notable experience to co-create the Teacher Education Institution Development Index, which became a cornerstone for strategic planning and institutional self-improvement among the LTTUs. This process not only enhanced the university capacity for governance and leadership but also introduced a culture of evidence-based management and peer learning that is being adopted more broadly in Viet Nam’s higher education sector.
Through technical support for teacher policy development, Viet Nam now has approximately 26,000 core teachers—one in each general education school—who are experienced and tasked with providing professional development support to their peers. The selection criteria and compensation for these core teachers are now defined through legal regulations, ensuring that they receive regular and sustained support, thereby enhancing education quality across schools.
The World Bank’s capacity building efforts extended to the development of digital platforms and blended learning models, which enabled the rapid expansion of continuous professional development for teachers in even the most remote and disadvantaged areas. By supporting the design and rollout of the Teacher Education Management Information System and a national learning management system, it helped institutionalize data-driven decision making and fostered a more inclusive and equitable approach to teacher training. The World Bank’s support helped bring together diverse stakeholders to form communities of practice and research groups, strengthening professional networks and promoting the exchange of innovative practices.
These indirect achievements have had a lasting impact, as the systems, tools, and collaborative mechanisms established with World Bank support continue to underpin Viet Nam’s ongoing education reforms and institutionalization of school-based continuous professional development. They have also catalyzed broader systemic change, embedding a culture of continuous improvement and collaboration. As the single most important school-based factor influencing student learning and success, investments in teacher capacity have enabled Viet Nam to build foundational skills and capacities needed for jobs.
Lessons Learned
While much progress has been made in tackling learning poverty through effective teaching and student support, gaps remain in the WBG’s institutional approach. A recent Independent Evaluation Group (IEG) report found that while support for structured pedagogy and targeted teaching is evidence based, the World Bank has often emphasized in-service teacher training without consistently tracking its impact on teaching practices. Initial teacher education has also received less attention, with a focus on coursework over practical experience and quality assurance.
A more comprehensive approach to teacher preparation and development. A more holistic approach to teacher preparation and development is needed, addressing the full teacher career lifecycle—from recruitment and preparation to ongoing professional development and career progression—to achieve sustained improvements in teaching quality and foundational learning. The World Bank has developed a framework for strengthening and supporting a pipeline of teachers for improved student learning to guide future lending and advisory operations by highlighting key intervention areas such as attracting and selecting future teachers, improving initial teacher education programs, and building leadership and flexible career pathways within the teaching profession.
Embedding evidence and quality assurance. An inaugural global study of initial teacher education identifies key principles and policy actions, informing the WBG’s recently launched Leadership for Progress in Foundational Learning capacity building program and the basis of an international knowledge exchange on teacher preparation and development. Building on these efforts, it could further tackle complex development challenges by harnessing the combined strength of its institutions in coordinated and integrated support to country efforts, addressing the findings of the IEG evaluation as a priority.
Mainstreaming ed-tech lessons. IFC’s ed-tech investments, aimed at promoting quality improvements in education, skills development, and jobs and productivity gains through technology, have yielded valuable lessons on what works—and what does not—when adopting digital tools to address learning and jobs gaps. However, these lessons have not yet been fully mainstreamed across all IFC Education clients, nor have they been widely shared across World Bank operations involving public education institutions. This is a potential area for further improvement and action under a One-WBG approach.

title: Driving Health System Reform for Healthier Lives
body:
Summary
The Challenge
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
Country Examples
Lessons Learned
Results Narrative
Healthier Lives
Driving Health System Reform for Healthier Lives
Credit: ©A'Melody Lee
Summary
The World Bank Group (WBG) is helping countries tackle systemic challenges—such as weak governance, fragmented service delivery, and limited regulatory and workforce capacity—through institutional reforms that expand access to quality, affordable care. Drawing on lessons from Colombia, Côte d’Ivoire, Indonesia, and Morocco, it emphasizes sequenced reforms rooted in strong legal frameworks, sustainable financing, and inclusive workforce strategies. By aligning policy advice, financing, and partnerships, the WBG supports resilient health systems and accelerates progress toward Universal Health Coverage (UHC).
The Challenge
Despite progress in expanding health service coverage, over half the global population lacks full access to essential health services, and 2 billion people face financial hardship when seeking care. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), between 5.7 and 8.4 million deaths are attributed to poor-quality care each year in low- and middle-income countries (LICs, LMICs). The WBG works with countries to increase access to quality, affordable health services, supporting economic growth and job creation.
The WHO defines Health Systems Strengthening as the process of improving policies and practices so health systems can better meet population needs, especially for the poor and vulnerable. This involves enhancing six key building blocks: service delivery, workforce, information systems, access to medicines, financing, and governance.
Advancing health system reforms can be challenging due to multiple public and private stakeholders often unaligned with at times competing interests, underperforming public institutions, and weak governance. Key barriers include limited political will, capacity constraints, and complex accountability structures across levels of governments and agencies. Institutional reform—changes to laws, regulations, norms, incentives, and organizational structures—is essential to this process.
A weak regulatory environment hinders the health system’s performance. Strong frameworks are essential for enforcing quality standards, effective private sector contracting, transparent procurement, and sound governance, while also creating a business-friendly environment.
Reforms must be guided by strategies that meet evolving health needs. Many countries need reforms in health financing and financial protection, which require effective coordination between health and finance ministries to mobilize, pool, and allocate resources efficiently. While strong public financial management ensures predictable, transparent funding for both public and private providers, insurance mechanisms must align funding flows and maintain financial sustainability.
These institutional reforms must also address the health workforce, as worker shortages and skill mismatches hinder service delivery, while AI tools demand new competencies. However, reforms to build and maintain a skilled, organized workforce may face both political and economic headwinds.
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
The WBG leverages its financing, knowledge, and partnerships to help countries provide quality, affordable health services to 1.5 billion people by 2030, providing policy advice, technical assistance, and learning opportunities to support reforms that expand access to essential services, including from the private sector.
Building capacity: The Health Systems Flagship Program enhances capacity (policy makers, practitioners, and WBG staff) to navigate health system reforms. Its goal is to help LICs and LMICs achieve UHC through more resilient and effective health systems. A forthcoming initiative—Tokyo UHC Hub—aims to strengthen the capacity of ministries of finance and health, fostering greater collaboration, supporting in-country capacity building, and promoting local leadership in reform implementation. It is part of a broader strategy to build sustainable, in-country capacity for health system transformation across LICs and LMICs.
Empowering reforms: The WBG combines tailored technical assistance with knowledge, analytics, and policy dialogue to help countries overcome reform constraints. It informs operations and country-led reform efforts—enhancing regulatory oversight, strengthening the implementation of national health insurance, improving quality standards of care, and designing governance mechanisms for private sector contracting. IFC complements this with advisory support for private providers that promote service quality, digital transformation, ethical care, decarbonization, and operational excellence—many of which are also applicable to the public sector.
Policy support: The WBG helps governments advance reforms for sustainable health financing, improved access and quality of care, a stronger health workforce, and sector regulation. Encouraging integration, it promotes equitable and efficient resource allocation and public-private collaboration while mobilizing investments to close health service gaps. The WBG also helps countries develop national health compacts as strategic roadmaps to support priority reforms, align domestic and external resources, and galvanize political leadership, among others. Commitments will be announced at the Tokyo Forum (December 2025).
Partnerships for impact: Bringing its convening power, financing, analytics, and deep policy engagement, the WBG works closely with WHO, Gavi, and the Global Fund to help countries address coordination and capacity constraints by aligning investments and supporting co-financing country reforms, reducing fragmentation and inefficiency. It is also strengthening partnerships with other global health actors like foundations to maximize synergies.
Healthy Systems: Unlocking Jobs Through Reform
Institutional health system reforms lay the groundwork for a more inclusive and resilient labor market through:
Expansion and upskilling of the health workforce: Workforce modernization is a priority, as digital health and AI integration requires new skills. To avoid job displacement, updating regulations is key. This includes task shifting, which allows health care workers at different levels to take on new responsibilities, and leveraging AI-powered tools for diagnostics and decision making.
Health ecosystem growth: In low-income countries, each health sector job generates an estimated 3.4 additional jobs in related industries (e.g., pharmaceuticals, digital health). Strengthening the health sector’s value chain creates jobs and economic resilience.
Tackling youth unemployment through the care economy: As populations age, demand for caregivers and support staff is rising. Targeted investments that equip young people with relevant skills can create quality jobs in caregiving and related services.
Gender-inclusive employment: The health sector offers steady employment and upskilling opportunities, especially for women, who comprise 70 percent of the global health workforce.
Redesigning service delivery models will help equip workers with future-ready skills, strengthen workforce planning and leadership—including increased role of women as leaders in health institutions—and forge adaptive partnerships with the private sector.
Economic and social impact: Healthier populations are more productive, while investments in health infrastructure and services stimulate local economies and generate demand for local labor and supply chains, especially in underserved areas.
Country Examples
Morocco
Pioneering Universal Health Coverage through Institutional Reforms
Credit: ©World Bank
Morocco is implementing institutional health reforms to achieve UHC, expand social protection, and build resilience. Recognizing weak human capital as a binding constraint to economic growth, the government announced an ambitious set of reforms in 2021 focusing on strengthening human capital as a key driver of inclusive economic development. The health system faced low and inequitable public financing, high out-of-pocket costs, fragmented services, workforce shortages, weak information systems, and stark rural–urban disparities—especially in maternal and child health—alongside a rising burden of non-communicable diseases.
The WBG has supported the government through policy design, health system strengthening, and frontline investments. At the core is a three-part IBRD-financed policy financing series, whose main pillar focused on overhauling the health sector and expanding access to quality, affordable care through institutional changes. The supported reforms expand compulsory health insurance to all Moroccans, replacing a fragmented system with the unified Assurance Maladie Obligatoire (AMO-Tadamon) and enabling the poorest to access equal benefits, including private sector services.
Morocco’s reforms centralized insurance management under the National Social Security Fund and created new regulatory agencies—The National Health Authority (Haute Autorité de Santé), the National Medicines Agency, and the Blood Agency—to improve strategic purchasing, quality, equity, and transparency, with WBG support through policy dialogue and technical assistance.
It also addresses workforce bottlenecks by expanding the pipeline of doctors, nurses, and midwives and making public health careers more attractive. By exempting health workers from civil servant law and establishing a more flexible employment framework, the reform paves the way for performance-based pay.
Morocco is also creating a deconcentrated delivery model and integrated health information systems. Driven by a reform program to strengthen governance, workforce capacity, and service delivery, the country is upgrading infrastructure and creating Territorial Health Groups (Groupements Sanitaires Territoriaux) to bring services closer to communities. Nearly half of all primary care facilities have been rehabilitated, and seven university hospitals are under construction.
Though ongoing, the reform now rests on solid foundations: a legal framework for universal coverage, a new service delivery model, and upgraded infrastructure.
Côte d’Ivoire
Rapid Progress on Universal Health Coverage through Digital and Policy Innovation
Credit:  ©World Bank
Côte d’Ivoire has advanced UHC through transformative institutional reforms and rapid scale up of the Couverture Maladie Universelle (CMU), the primary health insurance mechanism. Supported by WBG instruments, the country enrolled 68 percent of its 30-million population into CMU in under two years. Daily enrollment surged from approximately 200 to over 30,000 people, thanks to a decentralized, biometric, and mobile system.
A policy lending series, including Côte d’Ivoire's first investment-focused operation, enabled key legislative and regulatory changes. While mandatory enrollment was adopted, the CMU social security number—now Côte d’Ivoire’s most widely issued government ID—was introduced to facilitate patient tracking. Additionally, a Multiphase Programmatic Approach, through an integrated early childhood program, advanced a sequenced reform agenda, allowing continuous learning and scaling of innovations.
With WBG support, performance-based financing was rolled out across 113 health districts and over 3,100 facilities, with national agencies handling verification for better accountability. Also, access to primary health care was promoted through full coverage in rural areas and reduced co-payments in urban settings.
While support for the electrification of health facilities has further strengthened the service delivery quality, a new cadre of “welcome agents” has improved patient experience, creating over 5,000 jobs for the youth. Côte d’Ivoire is also rapidly digitalizing its health sector—350 facilities have already been upgraded. Addressing the needs of informal sector workers, the government recently implemented measures such as premium waivers, new financing mechanisms, and targeted subsidies.
Further, the WBG through IFC supported the government in developing a public-private partnership for diagnostic services, providing transaction advisory and legal and financial structuring. IFC is also working on an initial assessment of how the government can best engage the private sector.
These achievements underscore Côte d’Ivoire’s rapid progress toward UHC, setting a precedent for other countries pursuing ambitious health reforms.
Colombia
Strengthening Resilience in the Face of Crisis
Credit: ©Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency
The COVID-19 pandemic severely impacted Colombia and its capital Bogotá. By February 2022, the country had reported over 6 million COVID-19 cases and 138,000 deaths. Bogotá, with over 8 million people, experienced higher infection rates and major outbreaks compared to the national average. Colombia’s GDP fell by an estimated 6.8 percent in 2020 before rebounding to pre-crisis levels in 2021.
To address these challenges, MIGA issued a guarantee on a COP 1 trillion (about $254 million) loan to Bogotá—issued by Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria, S.A of Spain—for up to seven years. The loan aimed to enhance Bogotá’s COVID-19 response and related health care infrastructure. The guarantee enabled Bogotá to access international financing, diversify funding sources, and strengthen its health care system’s resilience.
While Colombia’s publicly funded, decentralized health care system covers nearly 98 percent of the population, COVID-19 exposed several vulnerabilities—from lack of intensive care unit beds to limited laboratory capacity. The MIGA-guaranteed loan aimed to support Bogotá’s response to the pandemic by funding COVID-19 testing, personal protection equipment, and other mitigation resources. It also helped upgrade Bogotá’s health care services, expand primary and specialized care, and strengthen Bogotá’s Medical Emergency Information System. The guarantee enabled Bogotá to deploy 105 new ambulances and 155 mobile medical teams. It also supported the renovation of hospitals and labs, helping overcome infrastructure and information system constraints. Without the WBG guarantee, Bogotá may have faced delays and higher costs in financing, leaving its health system vulnerable and limiting its capacity to coordinate care, expand services, and modernize emergency response and health information systems.
The project aligned with the WBG’s Fast Track COVID-19 Facility, which assisted member countries in addressing the global pandemic and its impacts. It also aligned with MIGA’s COVID-19 response package, which used guarantees to raise financing for medical goods, services, infrastructure, and system upgrades.
Indonesia
Transforming Health Systems for a More Resilient Future
Credit: ©World Bank
In 2022, Indonesia’s Ministry of Health launched the Health System Transformation Agenda to advance UHC, followed by the ‘Quick Win’ programs in 2024 with similar goals. The WBG has been a key partner in supporting these reforms for a more resilient health care system in Indonesia.
A World Bank-led partnership with the Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank, Islamic Development Bank, and the Asian Development Bank, totaling $4 billion, is Indonesia’s largest health investment. It supports upskilling the health workforce, modernizing facilities, and fostering public-private collaborations, working with over 300,000 primary care facilities, 560 hospitals, and 230 laboratories nationwide.
The World Bank is working closely with the Ministry of Health to ensure value-for-money while encouraging investment in local manufacturing and high-end biomedical engineering. IFC has supported Indonesia’s vaccine, pharmaceutical, and MedTech sectors, including a $15 million equity investment in Tawada Healthcare, an Indonesian medical device distributor.
Through an ongoing tuberculosis (TB) program, World Bank support has helped shift care for TB to the primary level, where Indonesia remains an outlier among high TB burden countries, with many cases treated in hospitals, which is disproportionally costly.  Notably, TB case notifications increased from 384,000 in 2020 to 804,000 in 2023. The WBG’s support for reforms to shift TB care to the primary level backs the government’s goal of reducing incidence by 50 percent by 2030.
The government, with World Bank support, is also working to reduce stunting, which remains widespread despite dropping from 30.8 percent in 2018 to 19.8 percent in 2024. The second phase of a program supports multisectoral interventions under the national stunting strategy, focused on delivering quality services to priority households and villages.
As unprecedented health insurance reforms now cover over 223 million people, reducing out-of-pocket spending by over 20 percent over the last decade, the WBG’s strategic investments, policy support, and partnerships are helping Indonesia build a resilient, equitable health system.
In the absence of coordinated reforms, the country’s health system could have remained siloed and under-resourced, limiting its ability to respond to TB, stunting, and other persistent challenges.
Lessons Learned
Achieving sustainable health system reforms requires a strategic, phased approach grounded in government commitment and a clear vision. Not all reforms have been equally successful; in some cases, limited political will or fragmented governance structures have slowed progress. The WBG’s experience in Morocco, Côte d’Ivoire, Colombia, and Indonesia shows that lasting change depends on broad, well-sequenced sectoral reforms rather than isolated interventions.
Successful reform efforts typically start with solid legal and regulatory foundations, enabling progress in health financing, service delivery, workforce development, and digital systems. Policy-based instruments help shape governance frameworks, while complementary health system projects can target specific challenges and build implementation capacity.
The Country Opinion Survey data reveal that WBG policy advice influenced health policy in over half of the countries surveyed in FY25, especially in health financing, insurance design, and workforce regulation.
Access to timely data and institutional support are critical for priority setting and capacity building. Early integration of analytics helps governments and partners identify challenges and design critical reforms to address bottlenecks in delivering quality, affordable health services to people.
Predictable, coordinated financing across instruments underpins sustainable health system reforms. Blending long‑term budget support, health systems–strengthening operations, and private investment enables a coherent, systemwide response for greater impact.
A unified “One World Bank Group” approach is key to advancing complex reforms. By fostering close collaboration among the World Bank, IFC, and MIGA, the WBG can create opportunities to enhance complementarity between IDA/IBRD lending operations, IFC investments and advisory, and MIGA guarantees to meet both public and private sector needs.

title: Strengthening Capacity to Implement Tax Policy and Debt Management Reforms
body:
Summary
The Challenge
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
Country Examples
Lessons Learned
Results Narrative
Effective Macroeconomic and Fiscal Management
Strengthening Capacity to Implement Tax Policy and Debt Management Reforms
Credit: @K M Asas
Summary
This Results Narrative highlights how the World Bank supports job creation by addressing data and institutional gaps in tax and debt policy. In the Republic of Congo, Egypt, Madagascar, Mauritania, and the Philippines, Bank interventions—ranging from public finance reviews to technical assistance and policy actions—strengthened debt transparency, improved tax incentive frameworks, and enhanced fiscal risk management. These reforms broadened tax bases and reduced borrowing costs, unlocking fiscal space for infrastructure, health, and education investments that drive inclusive growth.
The Challenge
High public debt, persistent fiscal deficits, and rising interest rates are straining government budgets more than ever. In October 2024, 55 percent of IDA countries assessed under the Debt Sustainability Framework for Low-Income Countries were either at high risk or already in debt distress—up from 52 percent in 2019. Since 2022, IDA countries have faced record high debt service payments; over 8 percent of GDP in 2024—around half of their tax revenues. The more a country spends on debt service, the less it can invest in infrastructure, health, and education—sectors critical for private sector growth and job creation. In low- and lower-middle income countries, tax revenues as a share of GDP have been stagnant for a decade at about 10.3 percent and 16.7 percent.
Limited political appetite for reform, weak institutions, and challenges in building consensus around sensitive fiscal reforms pose major obstacles to addressing fiscal imbalances. Data and knowledge gaps—especially poor-quality or unavailable information on tax expenditures and debt—hinder informed policy making and constrain sustainable borrowing strategies.
In many countries, tax expenditures—exemptions, deductions, and zero rates—lack clear rationale and policy objectives, success criteria, or cost-benefit assessments. While governments use them to offset limited public spending and attract investment, they are often ineffective, redundant, and misaligned with revenue goals. Without regular evaluation—and under mounting political pressure to expand concessions—they often multiply and persist, even when they no longer serve their intended purpose. For example, effective corporate income tax (CIT) rates—the share of income that corporations pay in taxes after accounting for all deductions and exemptions—have fallen. In Sub-Saharan Africa, CIT incentives quadrupled between 2009 and 2020. Inefficient tax expenditures are estimated to cost developing countries an average of three percent of GDP.
Similarly, incomplete information on public debt—especially subnational and state-owned enterprise (SOE)—creates challenges. With a tighter financing environment, most developing economies now rely on off-budget and more complex borrowing arrangements, complicating exposures assessments.
Bridging these knowledge gaps is essential to support evidence-based policy making, promote transparent and accountable fiscal systems, and enable reforms that reduce deficits and allow countries to invest more in health, education, and infrastructure.
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
The World Bank helps governments overcome data and knowledge gaps and strengthen capacity to implement tax policy and debt management reforms. Key elements of this approach include:
Public finance reviews (PFR): PFRs serve as the World Bank’s primary tool for generating knowledge and assessments that can drive evidence-based fiscal policy making. These reviews highlight fiscal costs, distortions, inequities, and actual versus intended impacts of tax expenditures. PFRs also provide a set of policy recommendations with estimates of their revenue or cost-saving effects. This information can enhance policy dialogue, help decision makers evaluate tax expenditures on clear metrics of cost and impact, and directly inform lending operations in support of reforms.
Legal and institutional framework and capacity strengthening: The World Bank provides targeted technical assistance and training in debt management to strengthen institutional capacity and help governments use debt and tax data more effectively in policy formulation and reform planning. On the debt management side, this includes support to strengthen legal and institutional frameworks and initiatives to promote debt transparency through improved data recording, monitoring, and public disclosure, as well as debt-related contingent liabilities and other fiscal risks. On the tax side, assistance helps governments analyze tax incentives, improve transparency, and strengthen institutions—broadening the tax base and expanding fiscal space.
Diagnostics and debt management strategies: The World Bank helps identify current debt challenges through diagnostic work, such as the Debt Management Performance Assessment, and by developing tailored debt reform plans, which often include support for fiscal risk management and assessment tools and medium-term debt management strategies. These plans outline technical assistance to improve capacity and reduce borrowing costs.
Policy performance actions (PPAs): Under the Sustainable Development Finance Policy (SDFP), all IDA countries undergo an annual screening for debt-related vulnerabilities. Countries with elevated and moderate risk prepare PPAs focusing on identifying and supporting reforms to mitigate their vulnerabilities by improving debt transparency, debt management, and fiscal sustainability.
In addition, efforts to improve the quality and quantity of tax collection include base-broadening tax policy reforms and strengthened tax administration, such as improved compliance management and digitalization.
How Fixing Data and Knowledge Gaps in Tax and Debt Policy can Drive Job Growth
Improving data and closing knowledge gaps in tax and debt policy is not just a technical exercise—it’s a catalyst for inclusive job creation. By addressing these gaps, countries can:
Broaden the tax base and increase public revenue by advancing tax policies that promote efficiency and equity.
Develop and implement evidence-based debt strategies that reduce borrowing costs, freeing up more money for public investments.
Strengthen institutions to better coordinate policy across ministries and align spending with national development priorities.
The resulting fiscal space allows governments to fund labor-intensive infrastructure projects, expand education and health services, and provide a stable macroeconomic environment that attracts private investment—all of which are critical for creating more and better jobs.
Country Examples
Madagascar
Enhancing Madagascar's Debt Sustainability
Credit: ©Adobe Stock
Since  the COVID-19 pandemic, Madagascar’s debt sustainability has significantly deteriorated. The Joint Bank Fund Debt Sustainability Analysis reclassified the external risk from low to moderate risk of debt distress due to expansionary fiscal responses and contingent liabilities, particularly from SOEs.
The fiscal and debt vulnerabilities exposed several structural constraints, including weak domestic resource mobilization, limited capacity to monitor and mitigate fiscal risks, underdeveloped institutional frameworks for debt and cash management, and poor data coverage and fiscal transparency, which hindered timely risk assessments and effective debt management strategies.
In response, the Government of Madagascar, with support from the World Bank and other development partners, implemented a series of reforms. The World Bank’s intervention focused on strengthening institutional capacity through training and technical assistance in debt management strategies and fiscal risk management, complemented by PPAs. This improved tax expenditure reporting and debt management strategies, increasing the tax-to-GDP ratio from 9.5 percent in 2020 to 11.2 percent in 2023.
Madagascar also established a legal and institutional framework to manage fiscal risks from SOEs, enabling the first credit risk assessment of two troubled SOEs—electricity and airline companies. These reforms enhanced fiscal discipline and policy credibility.
Without technical assistance, Madagascar would likely have faced further deterioration in its debt metrics. SOEs could pose fiscal risks if not adequately monitored, especially when they provide essential services or operate in strategic sectors. For instance, auditing the troubled water and electricity company JIRAMA’s financial statement for the first time enabled budgetary provisions to mitigate potential fiscal risks. Madagascar’s experience shows the importance of targeted technical assistance and institutional reform in strengthening fiscal frameworks and building capacity for sustainable public debt management. Ongoing reforms aim to develop the domestic market, improve cash management, and enhance debt strategies to strengthen borrowing practices and reduce costs.
Mauritania
Strengthening Mauritania's Debt Management Framework
Credit: ©Adobe Stock
Facing critical debt management challenges—including weak institutional capacity, fragmented legal frameworks, and inadequate reporting systems—the Government of Mauritania has engaged the Debt Management Facility (DMF), a multi-donor trust fund jointly administered by the World Bank and IMF, to align its debt management framework and practice with international standards.
In response to requests, the World Bank, jointly with the government and partners, has engaged in a fast-paced programmatic technical assistance that has led to advancements in Mauritania’s debt management practices. Since 2023, this engagement has resulted in significant progress, including (i) diagnostic work conducted to identify critical areas for improvement in debt reporting and debt management; (ii) a debt management reform plan developed to support the financial management of Mauritania’s national development strategy; and (iii) capacity building initiatives implemented to ensure adequate execution of planned reforms and improved debt management practices.
Upcoming efforts will further strengthen the current institutional framework around debt management by drafting a debt management law, increasing analytical capabilities, and strengthening operational procedures around debt management.
In 2025, a milestone was achieved with the finalization of the guarantee framework, which outlines the steps for the government and public entities to request and implement financial guarantees. Looking ahead, medium-term results include submission of the draft debt law to Parliament in 2026–27, enhancements in the debt reporting heat map, and improvements in governance and legal framework for borrowing policies conforming to international standards.
These reforms are integral to Mauritania’s implementation of its national development strategy, which focuses on increasing social and infrastructure spending and improving governance. Effective revenue mobilization—complemented by efficient expenditure management from increasingly complex financing activities—is key to implementing the strategy, which fosters job creation.
Republic of Congo
The Republic of Congo’s Debt Management Reform Plan
Credit: ©World Bank
The Republic of Congo is grappling with severe debt sustainability challenges, with public debt increasing from 92.5 percent of GDP at end-2022 to 99 percent of GDP at end-2023, and the country classified as being "in debt distress” in the latest joint World Bank-IMF DSA. This crisis stems not only from excessive borrowing but also from deeper institutional weaknesses: unreliable data, mainly on SOEs’ debt and fiscal risks, weak oversight, and limited analytical capacity to manage public debt. These systemic constraints hinder the government’s ability to make informed fiscal decisions and maintain debt discipline, both crucial for improving fiscal capacity and enabling investments in critical sectors for employment generation.
In November 2023, the government adopted a Debt Management Reform Plan. The World Bank has provided technical assistance to prioritize reforms, including restructuring the Debt Management Office to align with international standards, strengthening debt reporting to enhance transparency, establishing oversight mechanisms for borrowing, and formalizing a cash management framework to anticipate liquidity needs and protect key expenditures. Debt reporting was also enhanced through SDFP PPAs to increase SOE coverage. These reforms directly address key challenges identified in the 2021 Debt Management Performance Assessment under the DMF.
Progress accelerated in early 2025 after defaults in the regional bond market in October 2024 and the suspension of IDA/IBRD disbursements due to arrears. Strengthening debt reporting and operationalizing cash management enabled arrears repayment to the World Bank, prevented further defaults, and safeguarded key expenditures. Efforts are underway to finalize a fiscal risk management framework and reactivate the National Debt Committee to oversee borrowing decisions.
Ultimately, Congo’s ability to restore debt sustainability and unlock fiscal space depends not only on curbing new borrowing but also institutionalizing data systems and decision making processes that allow strategic liability management. Maintaining the reform trajectory would help shift the focus from crisis response to proactive, knowledge-based planning.
Philippines
Tax Incentive Reform in the Philippines
Credit: ©World Bank
Lack of data and knowledge posed a major constraint to better policy making and tax incentive reform in the Philippines. Without proper monitoring and evaluation, based on a patchwork of laws, and implemented by many development agencies, the provision system was complex, opaque, challenging to navigate, and not informed by ex-ante cost benefit analysis. This created a system rife with incentives, with little information on which ones achieved their policy goals. To address this, with World Bank support through Development Policy Financing and technical assistance, in 2021 the government introduced the Corporate Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises (CREATE) Act, which enhanced monitoring and evaluation of incentives, increased transparency, introduced a cost-benefit framework to aid in decisions to grant incentives, and reduced incentives. As a result, in 2022, 79 percent of Investment Promotion Agencies adopted the new Cost Benefit Analysis framework, above the 50 percent target. This was contemporaneous with a reduction in revenue foregone from tax expenditures from 2.5 percent of GDP in 2020 to 1.9 percent in 2022.
The CREATE Act was informed by World Bank research on the impact of investment incentives, highlighting the essential role of knowledge in delivering impact in the space of tax incentives. Through the Act, information on the expected impact of incentives helps determine whether they should be introduced and ensures that those introduced are more performance-based, targeted, time-bound, and transparent.
Egypt
Development Policy Financing in Egypt
Credit: ©World Bank
Following the COVID-19 pandemic, Egypt has looked for strategies to reduce runaway debt, drive growth, and create room for higher social spending. Domestic resource mobilization is a core challenge to this. Egypt’s tax revenue has stubbornly remained below 15 percent of GDP, a level generally acknowledged as a minimum to support adequate investments in human and physical capital. Tax expenditures are a major source of revenue leakage and create distortions undermining growth.
The World Bank has been an active partner in addressing these issues. Through the 2022 Public Finance Review and 2021 and 2022 Egypt Economic Monitor, the World Bank built up a wealth of analytical insight and reform recommendations. These informed the selection of tax-related policy actions in a policy financing operation in Egypt, launched in 2024. The  intervention aims to increase tax revenues by (i) focusing on higher income tax receipts via effective payroll tax implementation; (ii) eliminating preferential tax treatment for state entities and ineffective tax expenditures; (iii) raising taxes on T-bills and T-bonds (Income Tax Law amendments); (iv) boosting Value Added Tax (VAT) receipts, including through better tax administration and e-invoices; and (v) ensuring higher development fees and cigarette price adjustments. These reforms are expected to have a sizeable impact on tax revenues, cumulatively increasing by 1.1 percent of GDP over three years, enough to reach the 15 percent threshold.
The World Bank is continuing to provide analytics and tools to inform the reform agenda. Reforms are not possible without prior information on which reforms are needed and what their impact will be. The World Bank has built an input-output table-based VAT model to enable quantification of revenue impacts of proposed VAT reforms. This model captures the indirect effects of VAT exemptions on intermediate inputs and includes a novel calibration process using industry-specific informality estimates obtained from labor market survey data. This model will support base-broadening VAT reforms going forward and is expected to help fine-tune policy before it is implemented, increase revenues, and avoid adverse impacts.
Lessons Learned
Countries face common constraints in strengthening tax and debt systems, but several cross-cutting lessons emerge from World Bank engagement. These include:
Stable policies outperform temporary incentives. Country examples such as the Philippines’ tax incentive reforms and Egypt’s efforts to broaden the tax base underscore the importance of stable, predictable tax policy and sound debt management to promote a more efficient and equitable fiscal system that supports sustainable long-term growth and reduces borrowing costs.
Data and knowledge are prerequisites for reform. The biggest risk to bringing about outcomes through filling knowledge and data gaps is that countries may face other constraints, especially political economy hurdles and low capacity. Madagascar’s and Congo’s improvements in SOE fiscal risk management and Mauritania’s debt reporting reforms, illustrate how tailored technical assistance and better data can strengthen institutional frameworks and policy execution.
Without support, countries risk fiscal deterioration and missed development opportunities. In the absence of Bank support, many countries would face continued deterioration in debt metrics—relying on costly debt, remaining vulnerable to rollover and exchange rate risks, and depending on regressive, ineffective tax expenditures to promote investment. Without intervention, the missed opportunity to invest in development priorities would continue to limit economic growth and poverty reduction.
Even advanced economies face data gaps. Persistent knowledge and data gaps affect all countries. Tools for identifying appropriate policy responses must adapt to development challenges. The World Bank regularly uses PFR and SDFP to help clients identify fiscal gaps, set reform priorities, identify capacity development needs, and align debt sustainability efforts with country-specific reforms, particularly through performance and policy actions in debt transparency, management, and fiscal sustainability.
Managing SOE fiscal risks is critical for debt sustainability. Experiences in Madagascar, Congo, and Mauritania show that establishing legal frameworks and regular risk assessments for SOEs can significantly reduce hidden fiscal risks and strengthen overall debt management. As more countries face rising contingent liabilities from SOEs, these offer a practical roadmap for others seeking to enhance fiscal discipline and transparency.
