category: Affordable, Reliable and Sustainable Energy for All
title: Energy Across Borders: Advancing Regional Energy Integration and Cross-Border Energy Trade
body:
Summary
The Challenge
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
Country Examples
Lessons Learned
Results Narrative
Affordable, Reliable and Sustainable Energy for All
Energy Across Borders: Advancing Regional Energy Integration and Cross-Border Energy Trade
Credit: ©EAP Collection
Summary
Regional energy integration expands access and resilience, but progress is often held back by weak institutions, misaligned regulations, and limited planning capacity. The World Bank Group (WBG) is helping address these barriers in Southern Africa, the Pan-Arab region, and South Asia—advancing planning, connectivity, and cross-border trade. Lessons show that deeper private sector engagement is needed to de-risk transmission projects and scale investment for sustainable impact.
The Challenge
The lifeline of a modern economy, access to reliable and affordable energy, reduces poverty, supports essential services—powering hospitals, schools, and businesses—and creates jobs. Yet many countries face strained power systems due to unreliable, difficult-to-afford supply, or rapidly growing demand for electricity, or both. Many of these challenges stem from poor planning, heavy reliance on imported fuels, underinvestment, aging network infrastructure, or overreliance on a single energy source.
Regional energy integration offers a promising solution. Integrated regional energy systems boost cross-border electricity trade and strengthen power networks, allowing countries to access a broader pool of generation resources. This can improve reliability, resilience, and energy security by reducing dependence on specific resources and accelerating the clean energy transition.
Cross-border power trade enables exports from low- to high-cost areas, expands market size, and supports economies of scale. It also helps draw financing for renewable energy projects and can create new revenue streams and jobs, particularly in smaller countries. Coordinated planning can further improve renewable energy integration, support regional trade, and reduce costs. Consequently, it can free capital for public services and business growth, driving job creation across key sectors.
Despite these benefits, regional energy integration has not reached its full potential. Multilateral power trading is complex, requiring alignment of political, technical, and institutional frameworks. Cross-border trade requires countries to share resources to enhance mutual energy security and rely on neighbors for some of their power supply. Moreover, enforcing payment discipline between countries is often hard.
These non-financial barriers—weak institutions, outdated plans, and poor utility coordination, among others—hinder regional power integration. Inconsistent rules and the lack of shared platforms and regulations further stall progress, resulting in sluggish cross-border trade growth.
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
The WBG works with governments and private sector companies—through financing, technical assistance, knowledge sharing, advisory support, and policy guidance—to support regional energy integration, fostering growth and jobs. Over the past decade, nearly $5 billion has been committed through IBRD/IDA, alongside financing and guarantees for private developers of interconnectors and export-oriented projects.
Institutional support: The WBG helps strengthen the institutional capacity of relevant ministries, regulators, system operators, and power utilities to manage the complexities of cross-border coordination. The WBG offers technical assistance, knowledge sharing, and targeted capacity building efforts that enable countries to align national policies with regional goals.
Policy and regulatory support: The WBG promotes policy and regulatory harmonization of national frameworks with regional standards, fostering a unified market and institutional readiness. It offers tools and training for ministries and utilities on systems and operational planning and advises regulators on market design, trading rules, and dispute resolution. The WBG also supports payment discipline and utility solvency, especially in countries with distressed utilities or limited tools for managing cross-border price risks.
Building consensus: As a neutral broker, the WBG convenes governments and other stakeholders to negotiate cross-border trade agreements and coordinate infrastructure development. It also facilitates regional platforms for cost-sharing, dispute resolution, and funding allocation to support implementation and reduce fragmentation.
Operational and implementation support: The WBG aids with investment plans, dispatch software, stakeholder consultations, and with developing trading rules and settlement systems to enable effective regional market participation. Through IFC, it offers advisory and upstream support for private transmission projects and independent power producers connecting to existing networks and supports least-cost electricity exports.
Supporting private sector: The WBG helps governments tender projects and supports private developers with risk mitigation tools—reassuring investors and enabling capital flow into domestic and regional energy markets for sustainable cross-border supply. Guarantees address risks, including transfer and convertibility, expropriation, breach of contract, war and civil disturbance, and off-taker defaults, encouraging private sector participation.
Cross-border trade is a key pillar of Mission 300, launched by the WBG and the African Development Bank to connect 300 million people to electricity in Sub-Saharan Africa by 2030.
Credit: Dana Smillie / World Bank
Powering Jobs
A more connected power system lays the groundwork for a more dynamic, inclusive, and resilient labor market through:
Infrastructure development: Building and upgrading cross-border transmission lines, substations, and regional interconnectors creates direct employment in engineering, construction, and logistics.
Operations and maintenance: The ongoing operation of interconnected systems supports skilled jobs in grid management, system operations, and maintenance.
Private sector growth: By lowering electricity costs and improving reliability, integration enables business expansion across industrial, agricultural, and service sectors—spurring new enterprise formation and job creation.
Renewables deployment: A more flexible and interconnected grid can absorb more variable renewable energy (e.g., wind and solar), which generates green jobs in manufacturing, installation, and system integration.
Public services and social impact: Stable electricity enables hospitals, schools, and local governments to expand services, creating jobs while driving social and economic development.
Country Examples
Southern Africa
The Southern African Power Pool
Credit: Trevor Samson / World Bank
Southern Africa has long struggled to optimize electricity trade due to fragmented planning, limited transmission infrastructure, and financially strained utilities. To address these, the WBG has supported the Southern African Power Pool (SAPP) in developing a more coordinated and resilient regional market.
Since 2014, the WBG has helped strengthen SAPP’s technical, financial, and legal capacity, supporting renewable energy integration, grid expansion, and harmonized planning among member utilities. It has also facilitated regional platforms for cost-sharing, dispute resolution, and infrastructure coordination—critical tools for reducing fragmentation and enabling implementation.
SAPP is now one of the most advanced power pools in Africa, with a competitive regional wholesale market that includes private generators and traders. Trading through SAPP’s centralized market has continued to grow, delivering lower costs and improved reliability. New independent power producers operating in countries with financially distressed off-takers can use the SAPP market to mitigate risks and improve project bankability.
The WBG has supported developing key regional projects—Mozambique-Malawi Interconnector, Tanzania-Zambia Interconnector, and a cross-border transmission and generation initiative in Mozambique supported by IDA, IFC, and MIGA. These investments have substantially improved access to electricity, with participating countries achieving an average electrification rate of 46 percent in 2021. This figure is expected to increase significantly by 2040.
To scale impact, in FY25, the WBG launched the Regional Energy Transmission, Trade, and Decarbonization (RETRADE) program, which provides a platform approach to support SAPP, combining technical assistance, policy dialogue, and enabling frameworks to unlock private sector participation in regional transmission infrastructure. By enhancing connectivity in countries that still rely heavily on fossil fuels, it is helping unlock access to low-cost, low-carbon energy across the region. RETRADE is estimated to save up to $42 billion in system costs by 2040 through economies of scale and optimal resource use.
Further, MIGA guarantees for private sector contracts with utilities across the region improve investor confidence and de-risk private sector participation in SAPP. This includes guarantees to BTE Renewables, Actis Investments, Distributed Energy Expansion and Absa Group, including all MIGA’s traditional political risk insurance coverage and providing reassurance for investor concerns. SAPP’s experience demonstrates how targeted support for planning, coordination, and institutional capacity can overcome structural barriers and deliver outcomes in energy access, cost efficiency, and resilience.
Pan-Arab
The Pan-Arab Regional Energy Trading Platform
Credit: Dana Smillie / World Bank
Energy trade in the Arab region has been slow to develop, held back by institutional fragmentation, regulatory gaps, and limited infrastructure. To help address these challenges, the WBG, together with the League of Arab States, launched the Pan-Arab Regional Energy Trading Platform (PA-RETP) in 2016 to support regional electricity and gas market integration.
Through PA-RETP, the WBG acted as a trusted broker, helping member states reach consensus on critical market agreements. As a policy and technical advisor, the WBG provided expertise on foundational aspects of market design, governance frameworks, and regulatory structures. It also convened knowledge-sharing forums, sharing international best practices from other regions such as SAPP and the European Union. Finally, the WBG served as a data aggregator, developing tools such as the regional power system model to pinpoint opportunities for cost efficiency, sustainable growth, and cross-border energy integration. Knowledge work—supported by PPIAF and ESMAP—demonstrated the economic benefits of regional integration, while further analyses laid the groundwork for expanded regional gas trade by examining pricing models, trade barriers, and global best practices.
Informed by PA-RETP studies, the WBG also supported priority projects, including the $268.4 million Tunisia-Italy Interconnector, which will link North Africa’s power systems with Europe, support renewable energy trade in line with Tunisia’s climate and development goals, and is expected to be operational by 2028.
The WBG’s technical assistance helped establish key governance and institutional frameworks for the Pan Arab Electricity Market (PAEM), including a secretariat, trading rules, transitional mechanisms, and regional pricing methodologies. While pilot trading programs have tested these frameworks, ongoing coordination is vital to address differences in regulation, pricing structures, and institutional arrangements. PA-RETP has supported political consensus and regulatory alignment through platforms like the Pan-Arab Energy Trade Conference and donor roundtables. In November 2024, 10 member states approved two foundational market agreements during the 15th meeting of the Arab Ministerial Council for Electricity, marking a turning point in regional electricity market integration.
In the next phase, the WBG will support PAEM with a sub-regional focus, prioritizing actionable steps aligned with regional energy goals. Renewed efforts will focus on operationalizing PAEM using existing frameworks to address governance, institutional capacity, and implementation gaps.
Estimated Size of Selected Regional Electricity Markets around the World [Source: WBG, IEA Database]
South Asia
South Asia Regional Energy Integration
Reliable electricity is essential for South Asia’s growth. Regional integration reduces the need for each country to build reserve capacity, enables more cost-efficient generation, and supports climate goals through greater integration of clean energy. Yet fragmented planning, limited institutional capacity, and misaligned regulations hinder cross-border electricity trade.
To address these barriers, the WBG helps countries across the region strengthen legal frameworks, build technical capacity, and coordinate regional planning. This includes substantial upstream assistance across the region, where it has advised on commercial structures, risk allocation, and financing modalities for export-oriented hydropower to enable greater power exchange.
In Nepal, the WBG helped establish Nepal Power Trading Company and incorporate cross-border trade provisions into the Electricity Law. It also helped structure Upper Karnali (900MW) to serve both domestic and regional markets, showcasing multi-country off-take arrangements. In Bhutan, the WBG has helped prepare trade-oriented hydro and transmission projects, advancing the Dorjilung Hydropower Project toward bankability. These efforts are advancing regional integration, with environmental and social safeguards in place and regional cooperation helping to manage downstream impacts and ensure mutual benefits.
To unlock Central Asia’s hydropower potential, the WBG has mobilized concessional financing and technical support for the CASA-1000 project, a collaborative effort between Central Asia and South Asia, expected by 2028. The project incorporates governance structures, commercial agreements, community benefit-sharing mechanisms, and infrastructure to support variable renewable energy integration, and will provide a large capacity data highway to link participating countries through optical ground wire fibers running on transmission lines. Additionally, the Central Asia intervention includes dedicated community support activities.
Through the Electricity Market of South Asia (eMOSA), the WBG has supported systems planning, trade simulation, and regulatory design. It helped align legal and regulatory frameworks across Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Nepal, and Sri Lanka—paving the way for cross-border trade by creating a stable and predictable market environment, building institutional capacity, and establishing needed frameworks. It is also exploring MIGA guarantees to support private investment.
With WBG support, cross-border transmission capacity grew from 2.1 GW in 2015 to over 6.4 GW in 2024. The WBG, through MIGA, also provides guarantees to support private sector investment in the sector. A tripartite agreement now enables electricity exports from Nepal to Bangladesh via India. In Sri Lanka, the WBG is advancing  two major initiatives: a $150 million program that strengthens clean energy integration and grid resilience and the new Sri Lanka–India HVDC interconnection project, which will enable regional electricity trade and support decarbonization goals.
Lessons Learned
Lessons from WBG engagement across regions highlight what is needed to unlock the full potential of regional energy integration.
Institutional capacity is essential. Countries must invest in developing the skills and knowledge required by market participants and stakeholders. The WBG’s support builds institutional capacity to prevent fragmented energy markets and unlocks opportunities for access, growth, and job creation.
Regulatory harmonization is critical. Aligning national policies with regional standards—through a regional regulator or close coordination among national regulators—can help avoid inconsistencies and support more reliable and sustainable energy systems.
Risk mitigation remains a challenge. Government guarantees are often insufficient, especially where governance is weak or fiscal space is limited. In countries with limited access to financing, the WBG can help de-risk regional energy investments through multiple channels: IDA provides support to the poorest and most fragile countries; the WBG Guarantee Platform, housed at MIGA, offers partial risk, payment, and political risk guarantees; and IFC mobilizes financing partners, including through blended finance facilities.
Transmission projects require tailored financing. They often depend on more complex regulatory approvals subject to delays or revisions. Concessional capital with longer tenors or lower interest rates can help bridge financing gaps in many developing economies. A regional funding mechanism for early-stage project preparation—covering studies, feasibility work, and potentially procurement—could streamline cost-sharing and accelerate implementation. In SAPP, this is demonstrated through the Regional Transmission Infrastructure Financing Facility.
Utility revenue is foundational for scaling regional integration. A WBG database—tracking over 180 utilities in 90+ countries—shows that less than 40 percent of utilities generate enough revenue to cover annual operating and debt service costs. The WBG’s efforts to enforce payment discipline and promote cost reflective tariffs are critical for stable cross-border trade.
Private sector engagement is vital. Through models like build-own-operate-transfer and concessions, it finances and operates cross-border infrastructure linked to industrial demand and exports. The private sector also drives cross-border renewable energy deployment through regional auctions and power pools, diversifying sources and lowering costs. To scale impact, governments must ensure transparent procurement, stable regulation, and fair distribution of cost and risk sharing among participating countries.
Strong governance and contract enforcement underpin cross-border trade. WBG interventions, including stakeholder consultations, help countries build these conditions and reduce risks.

title: Enhancing Policy and Regulatory Foundations to Increase Digital Connectivity as a Driver of Growth and Jobs
body:
Summary
The Challenge
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
Country Examples
Lessons Learned
Results Narrative
Digital Connectivity
Enhancing Policy and Regulatory Foundations to Increase Digital Connectivity as a Driver of Growth and Jobs
Credit: ©World Bank
Summary
Despite progress, digital connectivity remains constrained by market inefficiencies, weak regulatory frameworks, and limited institutional capacity—especially in low- and middle-income countries. The World Bank Group (WBG) supports reforms to address these barriers by strengthening policy environments, building institutional capacity, and fostering private investment. These efforts help close coverage and usage gaps, supporting the WBG’s target to enable 300 million more women to use broadband by 2030. They also reduce costs, and unlock inclusive job creation, as demonstrated in Comoros, Guinea-Bissau, Kazakhstan, and Rwanda.
The Challenge
Despite major strides in expanding internet access, roughly one-third of the global population remains unconnected—most of whom live in developing countries. This is due to two main factors: a coverage gap, with over 250 million people in low- and middle-income countries still living outside the reach of mobile networks; and an even larger usage gap, with 2.9 billion people living within covered areas of developing countries but without a mobile data subscription. Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia together account for more than half of this global usage gap. In low- and middle-income countries, where digital transformation can be key to unlocking jobs, economic growth, and better public services, such digital divide risks leaving populations out of development opportunities. Within countries, digital divides are often concentrated among disadvantaged and vulnerable groups, including women, rural communities, persons with disabilities, and minority groups.
Building supportive policy environments that encourage competition and private sector investment is key to addressing these gaps. In countries with large coverage and usage gaps, markets may be concentrated and inefficient or lack the regulatory frameworks and economic incentives needed to promote effective private sector participation. This can further deter investment in new or upgraded networks, limit innovation and service delivery, and lead to higher retail prices and lower quality for consumers.
Figures are based on GSMA data and WBG calculations, focusing on LICs and MICs, with population-weighted averages. 'Covered' refers to people living in areas with mobile internet access. Source: GSMA, 2024 (link)
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
Financing, strong policy and regulatory frameworks, and institutional capacity to address market concentration and inefficiencies are key to attracting private sector participation and investment in broadband infrastructure and in supporting long-term sector growth. In this regard, the WBG supports countries by:
Strengthening policy and regulatory environments: The WBG supports ICT ministries, agencies, and regulatory authorities on telecommunications policy and regulation, with a focus on sector taxation, spectrum management, licensing, and market competition. This includes tailored support for developing legislation and regulation that align national frameworks with global good practices. Knowledge spillovers from broadband expansion efforts in other regions have informed regulatory reforms and market development strategies, enabling adaptation of best practices to local contexts.
Building institutional capacity: The WBG provides targeted training, tools, and advisory support to build the capacity of digital ministries and regulators to implement reforms effectively. This includes support for spectrum auctions, infrastructure regulation, market assessments, competition enforcement, and public investment planning. Stronger institutions are better equipped to manage complex reforms, regulate effectively, and adapt policies to evolving market conditions.
Fostering private participation: The private sector plays a central role in financing and operating telecom infrastructure, accounting for the majority of global investment in the sector. The WBG enhances the private sector’s role in telecommunication markets through non-financial means, such as supporting market liberalization and the restructuring or repositioning of state-owned telecom companies. It also provides de-risking mechanisms such as guarantees or insurance to encourage investment. In addition, the WBG promotes policies that improve market functioning by enabling infrastructure sharing within telecom and across sectors. To facilitate effective and transparent implementation, it engages in regular dialogue with the private sector to understand and help prioritize the regulatory barriers they face.
How Digital Connectivity Drives Job Growth
Expanding broadband connectivity has been proven to improve employment outcomes. Studies consistently show that increased use of internet services positively impacts employment—both through spillover effects from broader GDP growth (Katz and Callorda, 2018) and through the direct impact of broadband coverage on wage and self-employment (Chiplunkar and Goldberg, 2022; Hjort and Poulsen, 2019). However, to ensure these benefits are inclusive and sustainable, complementary measures such as digital skills training, upskilling programs, and trust-building are essential, particularly as digital technologies continue to reshape labor markets and change the nature of work.
Digital connectivity can play a critical role in enabling labor market participation through interlinked mechanisms:
Enhanced productivity across sectors. Improved connectivity supports more efficient workflows, supply chain integration, and access to information, all of which contribute to increased output and competitiveness. It creates ripple effects across the broader economy, facilitating indirect job growth in key sectors, such as health.
Access to global markets. Broadband connectivity allows small and medium businesses to reach international markets, scale operations, and create jobs in digital commerce and services.
Improved job matching. Digital platforms enhance the efficiency of labor markets by connecting job seekers with employers and reducing mismatches between skills and opportunities.
Access to training. Increased access to online training and career development resources helps workers boost skills and improve employability.
Creation of new digital sector jobs. Broadband expansion paves the way for new business models and industries—such as e-commerce, fintech, and IT services—creating jobs that did not previously exist.
Increased demand for skilled labor. The digital economy generates demand for workers with skills in software development, data analytics, cybersecurity, and digital infrastructure management, resulting in higher wages and improved job prospects.
Shifting workers toward wage employment. Evidence from Tanzania and other countries highlights a strong link between broadband internet access and a range of positive employment outcomes, including shifts toward wage employment and increased female labor force participation.
Remote work and flexible employment. High-quality internet access facilitates remote work opportunities, expanding labor force participation and increasing wages, especially in digital jobs. The benefits are even greater for those facing social barriers to in-person jobs (e.g., women and caregivers) or geographic barriers to traditional employment.
Country Examples
Comoros
Expanding Market Competitiveness
Credit: ©Diana Styvanley
In 2013, Comoros’ telecom market suffered from a lack of competition and limited opportunities for private investment alongside the state-owned enterprise, Comores Télécoms. As a result, consumers faced high prices and unreliable service. At the time, only 40 percent of the country’s 800,000 people had access to mobile service, and less than 1 percent had broadband internet.
The WBG support aimed to improve the policy and regulatory environment through the IDA-financed Regional Communications Infrastructure Program (RCIP) and by providing advisory services to a second operator, Telma Comoros (now Yas Comores). The WBG helped introduce market competition by facilitating legislative and regulatory reforms, including the adoption of the 2014 Telecom Law, which introduced a licensing regime for telecom operators and enabled the entry of Telma Comoros in 2015. IFC debt financing enabled the rollout of Telma Comoros’ network and helped stimulate market competition. The WBG also provided technical assistance to strengthen institutional capacity, ensuring that regulatory bodies could effectively oversee and implement these reforms.
Together, the WBG’s interventions fostered a more competitive market, leading to expanded high-quality telecom services, a significant drop in wholesale internet capacity prices ($5,500 to $60), and a surge in internet subscriptions from 0.6 percent to nearly 66 percent of the population between 2013 and 2022.
Finally, WBG efforts supported key drivers of internet usage as well, such as the development of e-government services, private mobile money services, and digital literacy initiatives, which benefited uptake for citizens, especially women, businesses, and the government.
Guinea-Bissau
Unlocking Private Investment
Credit: ©Daniel Nogueira-Budny
Guinea-Bissau's telecommunications sector has long faced structural and operational challenges, including the bankruptcy of the incumbent GuineTelecom and its mobile subsidiary GuineTel. GuineTelecom held a monopoly over fixed broadband services for years, which contributed to a stagnant and underdeveloped market with limited investment.
To address these market challenges and support digital connectivity more broadly, the WBG has been implementing a comprehensive support program. Under a series of regional projects—first West Africa Regional Communications Infrastructure Project and then West Africa Regional Digital Integration Project—the Government of Guinea-Bissau enacted regulatory reforms to encourage private participation and investment, while also strengthening institutional capacity. These projects provided technical assistance for necessary reforms to enable a public-private partnership for deploying the Africa Coast to Europe (ACE) subsea cable landing station at the coast of Guinea-Bissau in 2023, giving the country access to a subsea cable for the first time.
The WBG is also playing an important role in the privatization of state-owned telecom companies GuineTelecom and GuineTel—initially under the World Bank project and now through IFC as the government transaction advisor. IFC is supporting the design and execution of privatization—including transaction structuring, competitive tendering, and sectoral advisory services, which are critical to enabling the successful transfer of assets to private ownership. In parallel, MIGA issued a guarantee to Sonatel SA for its investments in private operator Orange Bissau SA to support the operations and expansion of the telecom network in the country.
Through improved market functioning, strengthened regulatory framework, and enhanced institutional capacity, the country has improved its competition outlook and encouraged greater private sector participation in the telecom sector. In parallel, broadband penetration has increased—reaching 36.5 percent in 2024, up from 12 percent in 2020—and the ACE subsea cable has led to a sharp increase in international bandwidth usage. These measures are contributing to the broader goal of establishing a single digital market in Western Africa.
Kazakhstan
From Regulatory Reform to Better Service Delivery
Credit: ©Vladimir Tretyakov
Kazakhstan's digital market was facing a fragmented regulatory framework, including unclear rules on spectrum allocation and infrastructure sharing, limited institutional capacity that slowed reform and enforcement, and high barriers to entry for new operators and tower companies, which led to de-facto duopolies in many regions. The unclear rules on infrastructure sharing led to a lack of sharing agreements, driving up costs for operators to expand networks, particularly in low-traffic rural areas.
The WBG's approach has focused on addressing constraints such as limited institutional capacity and supporting the evolution of an enabling policy environment. Through technical assistance, the WBG has helped Kazakhstan implement key regulatory reforms, including holding a first round of competitive auctions for 5G spectrum, mandating active and passive infrastructure sharing between major mobile operators, and enhancing data protection through legislative reform.
These reforms are increasing broadband penetration and leading to improved digital services as well as enhanced quality and lower prices of telephone and internet services. By fostering a competitive digital environment, Kazakhstan is harnessing the potential of digital technologies to drive economic transformation and improve the quality of life for its citizens.
Rwanda
Operationalizing Transformational Policies
Credit: ©World Bank
Rwanda has emerged as a leader in digital transformation in Africa, thanks in part to WBG support. A One WBG team comprising specialists from IBRD, IFC, and MIGA delivered a Digital Economy for Africa diagnostic in 2019, which laid the ground for an IDA-financed Digital Acceleration Project in Rwanda (RDAP). A team led by IFC later supported the development of a new government broadband policy and strategy that was approved in 2022. This was informed by a deep analysis of the broadband market and accompanied by an implementation plan for key policy recommendations, including those on infrastructure sharing and spectrum management. The policy was key to unlocking the liberalization of the 4G market, which had been stifling broadband market development.
The public investment support channel, provided through the RDAP, has been instrumental in implementing many of the policy’s recommendations. This has been achieved through continued technical assistance focused on regulatory reform in areas such as passive and active infrastructure sharing, as well as spectrum management. It also includes forward-looking planning for 5G. The overall aim is to crowd in additional private investment.
Beyond policy and regulatory support, key activities financed by the RDAP include broadening access to affordable devices, implementing nationwide digital literacy programs, purchasing bulk broadband capacity for government agencies, schools and hospitals, and deploying core fiber networks along existing power lines to enhance broadband and support digital services market creation. Rwanda’s broadband policy also recommends further reforms (e.g., mobile number portability) to help increase competition in the mobile retail market.
Overall, the joint WBG engagement has supported—and continues to support—Rwanda’s dynamic digital transformation journey, helping the country build a more vibrant digital ecosystem.
Lessons Learned
This Results Narrative highlights key lessons for addressing persistent constraints and scaling impact across digital markets.
Fostering a competitive digital environment is essential to unlocking the full potential of digital connectivity. Open and competitive digital markets stimulate innovation, attract private investment, and drive down costs, making connectivity and digital services more accessible. Liberalizing adjacent sectors such as energy, transport, or data centers can further support market development. The WBG should continue supporting countries to reduce market entry barriers, strengthen regulatory frameworks, and promote fair competition—key steps to accelerate digital adoption, create jobs, and drive broader economic transformation.
Persistent bottlenecks in digital development require ecosystem-wide solutions. The combined force of WBG instruments—financing, technical assistance, and IFC and MIGA private sector engagement—has proven most effective in accelerating and scaling impact. This integrated approach enables the WBG to address constraints at multiple levels from enabling policy reforms and de-risking private investment to strengthening institutional capacity.
Sustained political commitment is a cornerstone of successful policy and regulatory reforms. While technical solutions and institutional capacity are essential, consistent and high-level political will is critical to champion reforms, maintain momentum, and signal confidence to the private sector. Reform momentum can falter, however, without continued leadership and institutional buy-in. Long-term engagement, as seen in Comoros, where WBG support began more than a decade ago and enabled market liberalization and broadband expansion, shows that consistent political will is key.
Digital regulation is always playing catch-up with the private sector. Rapid WBG engagement mechanisms are necessary to build capacity and help governments adapt to new technologies and trends. The WBG strives to deploy agile engagement mechanisms that include just-in-time assistance and facilitate peer learning to help regulators and relevant stakeholders adapt to evolving digital transformation.
Effective broadband markets and capable regulatory oversight and decision making are preconditions for the development of the broader digital sector—including digital ID, digital services, and digital entrepreneurship. Together, these are important drivers of job creation and growth. Investing in carefully designed technical assistance in this space has proven to be a smart investment for the development of the broader digital economy.
Progress in digital connectivity is not always evenly distributed. Urban–rural disparities persist, even as national penetration improves—underscoring the need for targeted efforts to reach underserved communities and ensure inclusive digital transformation.

title: Accelerating Inclusive Use of Digital Financial Services
body:
Summary
The Challenge
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
Country Examples
Lessons Learned
Results Narrative
Digital Services
Accelerating Inclusive Use of Digital Financial Services
Credite: ©World Bank
Summary
Digital financial services are constrained by fragmented systems, limited institutional capacity, and regulatory gaps. This Results Narrative shows how the World Bank Group (WBG) supports policy reform, institutional strengthening, and inclusive design to help governments build foundational digital public infrastructure. Lessons from Indonesia, Niger, Sierra Leone, and Somalia demonstrate how targeted assistance and public-private collaboration can expand access, empower underserved populations, and unlock pathways to financial inclusion and job creation.
The Challenge
In India, women receiving government benefits can now access payments instantly through a mobile phone, thanks to a combination of digital ID and fast payment systems that have drastically reduced delays and corruption. In Brazil, small business owners are using Pix—an instant, low-cost payment platform—to accept customer payments, pay suppliers, and expand their businesses with unprecedented ease. These examples highlight the critical role that digital public infrastructure (DPI)—foundational, digital building blocks designed for the public benefit—can play in expanding access to financial services and enabling inclusive economic growth.
Yet in many countries, a lack of DPI holds back governments and the private sector from delivering digital financial services. For example, more than 850 million people still lack official identification, often a crucial precursor to being able to access and use digital payments. Digital IDs—an example of DPI—offer a clear way to solve this constraint. Fast payment systems are another example of DPI that can accelerate the delivery of digital financial services. However, just 8 percent of lower-middle income countries have such systems in place.
Establishing digital IDs, fast payment systems, and other DPI is far from easy. Countries face significant challenges—from fragmented digital strategies and low institutional capacity to outdated legal frameworks and poor safeguards for privacy, security, and user rights.
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
The WBG’s approach to enabling inclusive digital financial services combines direct investments in foundational systems (such as digital IDs, fast payments, and real-time data exchange, which require significant policy coordination and technical expertise) with institutional strengthening, capacity building, and strategic partnerships—helping governments lay the groundwork for delivering digital financial services and accelerate financial inclusion.
Partnerships: The WBG leverages partnerships and global initiatives—including ID4D (digital identification), G2Px (government-to-person payments), and Project FASTT (fast payments systems)—to play a vital convening role in fostering shared ownership of digital agendas and provide policy guidance and knowledge exchange for the design and implementation of DPI.
Public-private engagement: Proactive public-private engagement helps avoid siloed, underused, or exclusionary digital systems, and enables the identification of regulatory bottlenecks early. The WBG works across its institutions—World Bank, IFC, and MIGA—to facilitate conditions for both public and private sector participation by promoting transparent procurement processes, open standards, and frameworks for data governance and data sharing.
Technical assistance: These initiatives offer operational and policy guidance, good practices, and research to support the governance, design and implementation of DPI, and help countries to design laws, policies, and regulations for the digital and financial sectors.
Strengthening institutional capacity: Government institutions often lack the mandate, resources, or technical know-how to lead the development of DPI and must be empowered to coordinate across sectors, drive reforms, and create an enabling environment for public-private dialogue. The WBG strengthens capacity, including clarifying roles and responsibilities, ensuring sufficient staffing and sustainable funding, and building dedicated units for operational implementation. This can help governments move toward digitally integrated and data-driven national transformation.
While DPI offers significant benefits for financial inclusion and service delivery, the WBG also recognizes and helps countries address risks—including exclusion of vulnerable groups, misuse of personal data, and cybersecurity threats—which are particularly acute in fragile contexts.
How Digital Financial Services Help Create New and Better Jobs
Digital financial services make it easier for individuals and firms to access credit, register businesses, receive payments, and participate in e-commerce. This opens pathways for self-employment and microenterprise growth, particularly in underserved areas. In many countries, the shift from cash to digital payments has supported a wave of fintech innovation and informal sector formalization.
In addition, cross-border interoperability of fast payment systems can help importers and exporters access new markets. For example, Thailand’s PromptPay has established cross-border payments with Cambodia, Indonesia, Japan, Lao PDR, Malaysia, Singapore, and Viet Nam—helping importers and exporters in all seven countries gain access to new markets and grow their businesses.
For small and medium enterprises, these digital innovations translate directly into business growth and job creation:
Access to finance helps entrepreneurs start or grow businesses.
Improved digital payment systems reduce operational costs and make it easier for customers to pay.
Digital financial services strengthen cash flow management and business resilience.
Rolling out digital financial services also generates direct employment throughout the digital economy and requires new skills and services to operate and maintain digital systems, boosting innovation and creating new high-skilled jobs.
Country Examples
Indonesia
Scaling Inclusion through Reform and Fintech Innovation
Credit: ©Amartha
Despite strong economic growth over the past decade, Indonesia grapples with significant challenges, including low financial inclusion and substantial financing gaps in sectors such as private sector development, infrastructure, and public services like education and health. Women entrepreneurs in particular face larger funding gaps, with many struggling to secure financing.
To tackle these issues, the WBG has supported the Government of Indonesia through capacity building, regulatory reforms, promotion of competition, support for women entrepreneurs, and the strengthening of financial infrastructure, thus contributing to a more enabling environment for digital financial services.
In 2020, the government approved a blueprint for payment system reform, and in 2021, it launched BI-FAST, the national payment system infrastructure operated by Bank Indonesia (the central bank) to facilitate retail payment transactions in real time, safely, efficiently, and any time. The World Bank supported this effort through policy financing, which aimed to foster a deep, efficient, and resilient financial sector. Supported reforms have led to 118 Indonesian banks and fintechs adopting the new Open API standard for payments, which is essential for fostering digital payments interoperability and enhanced access to financial services. They also helped lower transaction costs for both individuals and businesses, creating a more efficient financial environment, helping improve account ownership and usage of digital payments, especially by women.
Facilitating private sector participation, in 2023, IFC partnered with Amartha, a leading microfinance fintech platform providing digital financial services in Indonesia. This led to the development of an innovative funding solution to boost access to finance for women-owned microenterprises, with IFC structuring a $200 million securitization-based financing package. This innovative solution was the first women-focused securitization-based structure in Indonesia, with all proceeds used to finance women borrowers in rural areas. This partnership aimed to provide reliable funding to women microentrepreneurs, thereby easing issues like income inequality and lack of access to formal financing.
Since the WBG’s involvement, Amartha's disbursement to women entrepreneurs has more than doubled, highlighting the importance of addressing non-financial constraints and leveraging indirect pathways to support economic development and financial inclusion.
"We are grateful to have a strategic collaboration with IFC. Amartha's technology and our digital financial infrastructure were built to close the MSME (micro, small, and medium enterprises) financing gap. The digital economy market share in Indonesia is approximately 40 percent of total digital economy transactions in ASEAN. This is where Amartha's business model aligns with the government's goal of achieving digital financial inclusion. With this partnership, we can go the extra mile to reach remote villages and women-led MSMEs".
Andi Taufan Garuda Putra, Founder and CEO of Amartha
Niger
Expanding Rural Access through Foundational Connectivity and Localized Solutions
Credit: ©World Bank
In Niger, the WBG is supporting efforts to enhance digital inclusion and rural growth through access to foundational connectivity infrastructure, digital ID, and digital financial services.
Launched in 2020, the Smart Villages for Rural Growth and Financial Inclusion Project aims to boost access to mobile broadband services in rural regions and deliver digital financial services to underserved areas. It deployed mobile connectivity sites and established digital centers in villages that serve as hubs for digital transactions and skills training. A significant emphasis has been placed on developing a user-centric digital financial services platform tailored to the population’s needs.
By overcoming connectivity barriers, the project has facilitated the deployment of mobile broadband in 780 rural locations and the establishment of 123 digital centers. These centers have enabled many, including a significant number of women, to open mobile money accounts and make mobile financial payments. As of August 2025, the project contributed to the opening of nearly 8,400,000 mobile money accounts and over 151,000 people were enabled to make/receive mobile financial payments for the first time. This has led to increased financial inclusion, empowering women and other marginalized groups with greater economic opportunities and access to financial resources, ultimately leading to enhanced quality of life in rural communities.
In FY22, MIGA issued a guarantee to support investment in the Airtel Money Niger SA project (along with guarantees in 12 other countries across Sub-Saharan Africa), which provides mobile money services involving wallet services, bill payments, money transfers, and savings and microloans.
Sierra Leone
From Emergency Response to Systemic Reform in Digital Payments
Credit: ©World Bank
Since 2020, the WBG has played an important role in supporting Sierra Leone’s transition from manual, over-the-counter cash payments to digitally enabled delivery of social assistance programs. When the government launched emergency cash transfers in response to COVID-19, it marked the country’s first attempt to use e-vouchers for large-scale digital payments. However, challenges quickly emerged around the identification of beneficiaries, confirming payments, and exchanging data between key agencies to ensure effective last-mile delivery—highlighting critical gaps in the DPI ecosystem.
In 2022, the government requested WBG support to scale up the emergency cash transfers for a third round, targeting 35,000 vulnerable households in 11 district towns. This phase marked a turning point: for the first time, payments were delivered via mobile money. This was supported by the Sonatel Orange Telecom Project, with the support of a MIGA guarantee, which helped roll out new mobile banking services. As of April 2024, 99 percent of payments were successfully delivered digitally. This success laid the groundwork for broader reform. The government has since committed to digitally delivering future payments for the Green Public Works program, the regular national cash transfer, and the Economic Inclusion Cash Transfer.
Working through the World Bank’s Global DPI Program, integrated technical assistance was provided to guide this transition. From 2021 to 2024, the G2Px Initiative supported day-to-day troubleshooting on payment reconciliation; ecosystem assessments to inform payment design; geospatial mapping of mobile money agents; and readiness assessments of beneficiary communities. In addition, the ID4D initiative provided technical assistance through an ID4D Diagnostic, which helped engage with key government stakeholders, as well as commercial banks, mobile money providers, civil society and other potential users of digital ID and Know Your Client services in agriculture and social protection sectors. With support from Project FASTT, Sierra Leone launched the “Salone Payment System,” its fast payment system, in 2025. The system enables interoperable digital payments across banks and mobile money operators and facilitates innovation in the digital payments ecosystem.
Sierra Leone’s experience illustrates how targeted technical assistance, public-private collaboration, and support for foundational systems can accelerate the shift toward inclusive digital financial services.
Somalia
Establishing Identity Systems to Unlock Financial Access
Credit: ©Ministry of Commerce and Industry
In Somalia, approximately 80 percent of the population lack official proof of identity. In this context, DPI initiatives supported by the WBG are pivotal in enabling infrastructure for inclusive digital financial services. Given the Fragility, Conflict and Violence (FCV) related risks affecting the country, expanding access to DPI and digital financial services is key to strengthening crisis resilience, improving inclusion, and unlocking economic opportunities.
For instance, the Somalia Capacity Advancement, Livelihoods, and Entrepreneurship through Digital Uplift Project aims to support progress toward increased access to basic digital financial and government services, with a focus on entrepreneurship and employment opportunities for women. By laying the foundations for a scalable ID system covering Somalia’s entire population, as well as supporting the development of a digital ID verification system, the project addresses several non-financial constraints, including the lack of DPI and regulatory frameworks that hinder the growth of digital financial services. The World Bank also supported the launch of the National Payment System, further enabling the safety and efficiency of payment transactions. Before its launch, many transactions were settled in cash, needed to go through offshore institutions, or relied on bilateral arrangements between banks.
Beneficiaries present their ID cards at a NIRA registration site (Credit: ©NIRA)
Credit: ©NIRA
With support of the ID4D initiative, the World Bank has provided technical assistance to the newly established National Identification and Registration Authority (NIRA) on best practices for registration policies, ID credential design, ID verification and authentication approaches, and data protection and privacy. The project also focused on reforming business registration requirements, which simplified the process for entrepreneurs to start and manage businesses, thereby fostering a more enabling environment for financial and digital services.
The adoption of the National Identification and Registration Law the Data Protection Law, and the Digital ID regulations, have helped Somalia overcome important regulatory barriers. The country has been able to issue over 190,000 unique ID numbers as of June 2025. The newly established authentication platform has been rolled out to nine banks and, over time, is expected to lead to increased financial inclusion, particularly for women. Enhanced eKYC protocols have helped expand financing and economic opportunities for MSMEs. A follow-up operation was approved in May 2025 to further the development of private sector-led growth, including through DPI and financial sector reforms.
Lessons Learned
DPI has the potential to amplify the benefits achieved through digital financial services by accelerating financial inclusion and addressing existing gaps. WBG experiences offer insights into what works—and what remains uncertain—as countries navigate the complex and evolving process of building inclusive digital financial ecosystems.
Legal and regulatory foundations. Investments through World Bank, IFC, and MIGA operations need to be accompanied by continuous dialogue and comprehensive reforms to establish a robust legal and regulatory framework that supports the growth of digital financial services.
Upstream support and technical assistance. Targeted technical assistance supports upstream design and project implementation. The World Bank—through operational and policy guidance, good practices, research, upstream and just-in-time technical assistance, and peer exchange—provides a cohesive package of support for countries.
Collaborative ecosystem building. There is a need for more and better multi-stakeholder collaboration to ensure the effective functioning of the DPI and digital financial services ecosystems.
Through its Global DPI Program, the WBG leverages its convening power and thought leadership to forge strong partnerships with policy makers, the private sector, donors, UN agencies, academic institutions, and civil society organizations.

title: Women Entrepreneurship as a Source of Job Creation
body:
Summary
The Challenge
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
Country Examples
Lessons Learned
Results Narrative
Gender Equality
Women Entrepreneurship as a Source of Job Creation
Credit:  ©Dominic Chavez
Summary
This Results Narrative highlights how the World Bank Group (WBG) support for policy reform, institutional strengthening, and market creation helps governments address non-financial constraints—such as discriminatory laws, limited data, and restrictive social norms—that hinder women-led businesses. Drawing on lessons from Argentina, Armenia, Ethiopia, and Jordan, it shows how integrated approaches across WBG instruments can foster inclusive entrepreneurship, unlock job creation, and deliver more durable development outcomes.
The Challenge
Women entrepreneurs drive innovation and job creation, owning or operating over a third of small and medium enterprises (SMEs) in emerging markets and contributing significantly to economic growth. Globally, women-led businesses represent an estimated 25 percent of formal businesses. However, evidence shows women face barriers that are unique or more acute than those men encounter, hindering their ability to start, sustain, and expand businesses. Significant challenges continue to impede the efficient flow of capital to women-led businesses. In many countries, women more often than men cite access to finance as a major constraint and face higher collateral requirements and loan rejection rates. To address this challenge, the WBG Gender Strategy 2024-2030 Implementation Plan aims to help 80 million more women and women-led businesses access capital by 2030 through scalable solutions that tackle key funding barriers.
Women entrepreneurs often face an inadequate enabling environment, with discriminatory laws and policies reinforcing gender disparities. Nearly 25 percent of economies worldwide still limit women’s property rights, affecting their ability to use assets as collateral for business loans. Insufficient infrastructure (e.g., weak credit systems) is a further deterrent. These barriers are often more binding for women due to social norms and legal restrictions, and the lack of sex-disaggregated data hinders targeted interventions. Only 44 out of 143 economies provided sex-disaggregated data to the World Bank’s Entrepreneurship Database, reflecting a severe gap in such data.
Financial service providers are usually unable to serve women entrepreneurs effectively: products are not tailored to their size, sector, or collateral; institutions have limited risk appetite due to biases and lack of data; and weak business models limit market opportunities.
Women entrepreneurs also often lack access to capacity building opportunities, including training in record keeping or loan applications. Evidence shows women are less likely than men to have access to training, mentorship, or networks, and that social norms and time constraints further limit their participation. WBG efforts aim to shift competition from a constraint to a driver of inclusive innovation.
Indirect Pathways to Outcomes
The WBG takes a holistic approach to unlocking capital for women-led businesses, addressing both financial and non-financial barriers.
Creating enabling environments: The WBG helps countries strengthen legal, regulatory, and institutional frameworks to expand access to capital for women-led businesses. This includes embedding gender in financial sector reforms, strengthening financial institutions’ capacity to collect, track, and utilize sex-disaggregated data through initiatives such as the Women Entrepreneurs Finance Code, and addressing legal barriers to asset ownership and collateral access. Tools like the Women Business and Law database support these efforts. Finally, the WBG aims to expand financial infrastructure to enable alternative financing for women-led businesses.
Unlocking financing and institutional support: The WBG promotes innovative financial instruments that reduce reliance on collateral for women-led businesses. This involves working with financial service providers to design and launch gender-specific financing products and providing technical assistance to enhance their capabilities. The WBG promotes gender lens investing and fintech innovations, linking fund managers to women entrepreneurs through IFC initiatives like Invest2Equal and ScaleX. It also mobilizes private capital through gender bonds, guarantees, impact funds, and blended finance tailored for women-led businesses. The WBG expands investment and advisory support through platforms like IFC’s Banking on Women, offering technical assistance and scaling green finance, risk transfer mechanisms, and inclusive credit solutions.
Building capacity of women entrepreneurs: The WBG supports training, mentoring, and networking to equip women entrepreneurs with skills and resources to access capital. IFC initiatives like SheWins and  Sourcing2Equal connect them with financing opportunities, mentorship, and networks. The WBG also trains investors in gender lens investing while MIGA’s guarantees help strengthen financial and business skills.
Unlocking Job Creation through Women’s Entrepreneurship
Empowering women entrepreneurs is a promising strategy for driving job creation and economic growth. Long-run income per-capita would be almost 20 percent higher if women were employed at the same rate as men. In India, advancing female entrepreneurship and removing barriers to women’s economic participation could generate millions of jobs and nearly double the rate of female labor force participation.
Despite their potential, women-led businesses in emerging markets and developing economies face significant obstacles, particularly in accessing finance: more than 30 percent of the total gap in access to financial services is attributed to women-owned micro, small, and medium enterprises. This massive global credit gap is estimated at $1.9 trillion.  Furthermore, only seven percent of private equity and venture capital in emerging markets is directed toward women-led enterprises. This persistent financing gap highlights the urgent need for targeted interventions—both financial and non-financial—to address the obstacles that prevent capital from being channeled to women-led businesses.
By closing these gaps and supporting women entrepreneurs, countries can unlock substantial job creation, foster inclusive economic growth, and build more resilient communities. Investing in women’s entrepreneurship is not only a matter of equity but also a strategic imperative for expanding employment opportunities and driving sustainable development.
Country Examples
Ethiopia
From Mindset to Money: Integrated Approach to
Women’s Financial Inclusion
Credit: Binyam Teshome / World Bank
Over a decade ago, women entrepreneurs in Ethiopia faced significant barriers due to lack of tailored financial services. In response, the Government of Ethiopia—with WBG’s support—launched the flagship Women Entrepreneurship Development Project (WEDP).
This initiative (2012–2024) helped increase the earnings and employment of women-led micro and small enterprises by improving access to relevant financial services and strengthening the capacity of microfinance institutions to better serve women entrepreneurs. To build capacity, WEDP trained 32,000 women entrepreneurs using a tailored curriculum. A psychology-based training was introduced to strengthen both business skills and an entrepreneurial mindset, addressing a critical non-financial barrier: social norms and constraints that limit women’s ability to take on risk or pursue entrepreneurship.
Drawing on evidence-based recommendations from the Africa Gender Innovation Lab, WEDP also introduced psychometric credit scoring—an approach that assesses personal traits linked to a borrower’s ability and willingness to repay loans, enabling women without traditional physical collateral to secure financing. This approach can also be applied to other marginalized groups and lower-income borrowers, reducing credit market inequities and promoting greater financial inclusion.
The project’s impact was transformative. Beneficiary firms saw income growth of 67 percent and employment growth of 55 percent over three years before COVID-19. By empowering women entrepreneurs, WEDP showcases how strategic interventions can overcome persistent non-financial constraints to financial inclusion. Without it, women entrepreneurs in Ethiopia would likely have continued to face significant barriers to loans and business growth, missing opportunities for higher earnings and job creation.
Jordan
Legal Reforms and Social Norm Shifts to Enable
Women’s Home-Based Businesses
Credit: ©Mohamed Azakir
Jordan’s economy, with an average growth rate of 2.5 percent over the past decade, faces high unemployment (22.3 percent in 2023). At 13.5 percent, Jordan’s female labor force participation rate is among the lowest in the world. The country also hosts a large Syrian refugee population of 1.3 million people as of 2023. The influx of refugees has added pressure to the labor market, compounding existing barriers to women’s participation.
World Bank results-based financing supported upstream reforms and capacity building to enable women’s economic participation. This included helping establish a legal framework for home-based businesses, allowing women—especially those constrained by social norms—to formalize and grow enterprises from home. The program also provided targeted entrepreneurship support, including financial inclusion and training, and promoted enabling conditions such as childcare arrangements and a simplified legal framework for registering home-based nurseries. These interventions helped shift social norms and contributed to increased female labor force participation, demonstrating how the WBG’s technical assistance and policy support can strengthen institutional environments that unlock climate-resilient and inclusive economic opportunities.
By end-2023, over 4,900 new home-based businesses were established, more than 2,800 of them owned by women. This was driven by eased restrictions in sectors such as tailoring, food, and crafts, which traditionally employed many women.
The project also facilitated digital economic opportunities, training over 4,000 people and creating more than 3,200 jobs in the growing digital sector. This provided women with new avenues for entrepreneurship and helped them integrate into the formal economy.
Without WBG support, legal and institutional barriers to home-based businesses and childcare services would likely have persisted, limiting women’s ability to participate in the workforce and formal economy. As a result, thousands of women would have remained excluded from entrepreneurship opportunities.
Armenia
Mentorship and Market Linkages to Boost Women-Led Start-Ups
Credit: ©Armine Grigoryan
Societal expectations, biases, and limited access to training, mentoring, and networking have long hindered the growth potential of women entrepreneurs in Armenia. To address these challenges, the Government of Armenia, with WBG support, launched the Armenia Women Entrepreneurship Project.
Since 2020, the project has focused on improving the investment readiness of women-led businesses by enhancing competencies and expanding access to networks. The WBG through IFC provided individual and group mentoring, coaching, and advisory services to enhance negotiation skills and boost confidence. Networking events further helped women entrepreneurs build valuable business linkages. Over 700 women entrepreneurs have received skills training, with nearly 80 percent reporting they applied the skills they learned. Notably, 53 women entrepreneurs successfully raised capital, with 30 percent of them raising over $1 million in investments and supporting the creation of over 145 new jobs.
While supporting women entrepreneurs, the project also built the capacity of four local business intermediaries—Armenian Young Women Association, Small and Medium Entrepreneurship Development National Center, Fashion and Design Chamber, and the Business Support Office and Enterprise Incubator Foundation—to deliver women-focused programs. These intermediaries trained 600 women entrepreneurs, increasing the project’s impact. Although the project directly reached a modest number of beneficiaries, it sparked broader momentum for supporting women entrepreneurs in Armenia, inspiring the launch of similar initiatives like Accelerator and Bootcamps.
The project proved especially impactful during the COVID-19 pandemic, providing critical support to women entrepreneurs to sustain and grow their businesses. Several women-led businesses not only weathered the crisis but even grew—testament to the resilience that can be built with the right capacity support. Without this project, the momentum of Armenia’s entrepreneurial ecosystem, especially for women entrepreneurs, could have slowed significantly. They would have continued to face obstacles to becoming investment ready, limiting their potential to contribute to job creation and economic growth.
Argentina
Leveraging Blended Finance to Expand
Credit for Women-Led SMEs
Credit: ©Nahuel Berger
In Argentina, women-owned small and medium enterprises (WSMEs) face a large financing gap. Engagement with Santander Argentina shows that the WBG can amplify its effectiveness to close such gaps through guarantee products, advisory services, and strong collaboration with the private sector.
The WBG through MIGA issued a $200 million guarantee in 2022 and an additional $100 million in 2023 to Banco Santander for its investments in Santander Argentina, one of the country’s largest banks. These guarantees helped reduce risks for the bank, generating significant efficiencies, and thus enabling additional funds to expand lending to small businesses, including those owned by women.
As part of the program, Santander committed to strengthening its support for WSMEs, channeling significant funds to these businesses between 2021 and 2024. As an additional initiative, IFC provided advisory services via its Banking on Women program. This included helping Santander implement a comprehensive gender, diversity, and inclusion strategy internally and in its product offering. IFC also provided, together with Santander Argentina, a business training program that certified over 200 women-led SMEs and helped the bank create a gender framework for its debt capital market activities.
MIGA issued a $500 million guarantee to Banco Santander in 2025. Encouraged by its earlier success, Santander Argentina set new, ambitious goals to further increase loans to women-led small and medium businesses. By 2027, the bank aims to deliver about $1.45 billion in new lending and reach about 100,000 women entrepreneurs.
WBG guarantees and advisory support helped Santander Argentina scale its lending to women-led SMEs. This initiative highlights the importance of working with financial institutions to better serve women entrepreneurs and demonstrate the value of collaboration across the WBG to promote inclusive growth and women’s economic empowerment.
Lessons Learned
WBG experience across diverse contexts shows that when women-led businesses have greater access to capital, they generate jobs and drive inclusive growth. Achieving this requires stronger institutional and market systems. Three key lessons emerge:
Institutional capacity and ecosystem support are critical. In Ethiopia and Armenia, building the capacity of financial institutions and intermediaries expanded access to capital for women entrepreneurs. Technical assistance for lenders and borrowers unlocked new lending models and improved investment readiness, enabling women to start and grow businesses that create jobs.
Legal and policy reforms need implementation support and behavioral change. In Jordan, legal reforms for home-based businesses and childcare only delivered results when paired with simplified procedures and shifts in social norms. In Armenia, confidence-building and peer networks helped women entrepreneurs formalize, expand, and hire.
Guarantees and client capacity support can unlock scale. In Argentina, Santander’s experience shows guarantees de-risk lending and crowd in capital for women-led SMEs. Technical advisory and business training help women entrepreneurs access capital, supporting business growth and job creation.
These lessons highlight that supporting women entrepreneurs is a proven strategy to drive job creation, boost productivity, and reduce poverty. When upstream reforms and institutional systems are reinforced alongside targeted support, gender-focused investments deliver scalable and sustainable results for jobs and growth.
